17	  \,\colon\,
18	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
19	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
20	\]
21	The chain assembled across the four sub-build chapters is:
22	\begin{itemize}
23	  \item \texttt{RR.1} (\texttt{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}): the Weil-divisor
24	    group \(\Div(C)\), the degree map
25	    \(\deg \colon \Div(C) \to \mathbb Z\) (\texttt{def:divisor_degree}), and the
26	    degree-zero principal-divisor identity
27	    (\texttt{thm:principal_deg_zero}).
28	  \item \texttt{RR.2} (\texttt{chap:RiemannRoch_RRFormula}): the Riemann--Roch
29	    formula in genus zero \(\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1\) for \(\deg(D) \geq 0\)
30	    (\texttt{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero}).
31	  \item \texttt{RR.3} (sibling chapter, dispatched parallel iter-174):
32	    the line bundle \(\mathcal O_C([P])\), its global-sections identification
33	    \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P])) = 2\), and the corollary
34	    \texttt{cor:nonconstant\_function\_genus\_zero} producing a non-constant
35	    rational function $f \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P])) \setminus
36	    \bar k \cdot 1\( on a genus-\)0\( curve with a marked \)\bar k$-point.
37	  \item \texttt{RR.4} (\emph{this chapter}): the global classification ``a
38	    smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\)
39	    and a \(\bar k\)-rational point is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).''
40	\end{itemize}
41	This chapter consumes \texttt{RR.3} (the non-constant function) and produces
42	the Lean target \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1} pinned at
43	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean} line~290 and restated as
44	the interface block \texttt{chap:RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso} of
45	\texttt{AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex}. The \texttt{RR.4}-closure of that target
46	also drops the \texttt{sorryAx} propagation tagged on
47	\texttt{rigidity\_genus0\_curve\_to\_grpScheme} via
48	\texttt{thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}.
49	\paragraph{Scope of the \(\bar k\)-base classification.} The chapter restricts
50	to the case where the base field \(\bar k\) is algebraically closed. Over $\bar
51	k\( every closed point is \)\bar k\(-rational, so the ``\)C\( has a \)\bar
52	k$-rational point'' hypothesis of Hartshorne's Exercise~IV.1.3 is automatic
53	(the smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) is non-empty,
54	hence has closed points, all of which are \(\bar k\)-rational). The general
55	``\(k \to \bar k\) descent'' step ``genus-\(0\) curve over a non-closed \(k\) with
56	a \(k\)-rational point \(\cong \mathbb P^1_k\)'' is a separate sub-build hosted
57	in \texttt{Jacobian.tex} (the \texttt{genusZeroWitness.key} downstream
58	consumer) and is out of scope here.
59	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, \(\bar k\) is an
60	algebraically closed field and \(C\) is a smooth proper geometrically
61	irreducible curve over \(\bar k\). The Lean signature
62	(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RationalCurveIso.lean}) threads the
63	project's standing curve typeclass package:
64	\begin{quote}
65	  \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
66	   (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
67	   \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
68	   \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom]}
69	\end{quote}
70	matching the typeclass set used by the headline target
71	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1} in
72	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean:290--296}. The genus
73	\(g(C)\) is the one defined in \texttt{def:genus} of \texttt{Genus.tex},
74	namely \(g(C) = \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\) via the project's
75	\(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured sheaf cohomology
76	(\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genus}); the concrete projective line target
77	\(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) is the project's
78	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.ProjectiveLineBar~kbar} of
79	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects.lean}.
80	\section{The morphism to \(\mathbb P^1\) from two global sections}
81	\begin{lemma}
82	
83	
84	\leanok
85	[Morphism to \(\mathbb P^1\) from a globally generating pair of sections]
86	  \label{lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections}
87	  
88	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphismToP1OfGlobalSections}
89	  Let \(X\) be a scheme over a field \(\bar k\), let \(\mathcal L\) be an invertible
90	  sheaf on \(X\), and let \(s_0, s_1 \in H^0(X, \mathcal L)\) be two global
91	  sections whose common zero locus is empty:
92	  \[
93	    \{x \in X : s_0(x) = 0 \text{ and } s_1(x) = 0\} \;=\; \emptyset.
94	  \]
95	  (Equivalently, \(s_0\) and \(s_1\) generate \(\mathcal L\) at every point.) Then
96	  there exists a unique morphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes
97	  \[
98	    \varphi_{(s_0, s_1)} \colon X \longrightarrow \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}
99	  \]
100	  whose pullback of \(\mathcal O_{\mathbb P^1}(1)\) is \(\mathcal L\) and whose
101	  pullbacks of the standard coordinate sections $x_0, x_1 \in
102	  H^0(\mathbb P^1, \mathcal O(1))\( are \)s_0\( and \)s_1$ respectively. On the
103	  basic-open chart $D_+(x_0) = \mathbb A^1_{\bar k} \subset
104	  \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\( the morphism \)\varphi_{(s_0, s_1)}\( is the map ``\)x
105	  \mapsto s_1(x) / s_0(x)\(''; on the chart \)D_+(x_1)\( it is the map ``\)x
106	  \mapsto s_0(x) / s_1(x)$''; the two definitions agree on the overlap
107	  \(D_+(x_0) \cap D_+(x_1) = \mathbb G_{m, \bar k}\) by the cocycle identity
108	  \((s_1 / s_0) \cdot (s_0 / s_1) = 1\).
109	\end{lemma}
110	\begin{proof}
111	
112	
113	
114	\leanok
115	  This is the standard linear-system construction in the form of
116	  Hartshorne's Theorem~II.7.1 (``Morphisms to \(\mathbb P^n\)''), specialised to
117	  \(n = 1\). Mathlib (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) ships the corresponding API
118	  under the name
119	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} in
120	  \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.ProjectiveSpectrum.Basic}:
121	  \begin{quote}
122	  \small
123	  \texttt{def Proj.fromOfGlobalSections \{\(\sigma\) A\} [CommRing A]
124	    [SetLike \(\sigma\) A] [AddSubgroupClass \(\sigma\) A] (\(\mathcal A\) :
125	    \(\mathbb N\) → \(\sigma\)) [GradedRing \(\mathcal A\)] \{X : Scheme\}\\
126	    \phantom{}\quad(f : A →+* X.presheaf.obj \(\top\))\\
127	    \phantom{}\quad(hf : Ideal.map f (HomogeneousIdeal.irrelevant \(\mathcal A\)).toIdeal = \(\top\)) :\\
128	    \phantom{}\quad(X \(\longrightarrow\) Proj \(\mathcal A\))}
129	  \end{quote}
130	  Specialising to \(\mathcal A = \bar k[x_0, x_1]\) with the standard $\mathbb
131	  N\(-grading by total degree, the inputs are a graded \)\bar k$-algebra
132	  homomorphism \(f \colon \bar k[x_0, x_1] \to H^0(X, \mathcal O_X)\) and a
133	  proof that the irrelevant ideal \((x_0, x_1) \cdot \bar k[x_0, x_1]\) maps to
134	  the unit ideal of \(H^0(X, \mathcal O_X)\) (i.e.\ no common zeros). The
135	  resulting morphism lands in $\mathrm{Proj}\,\bar k[x_0, x_1] = \mathbb
136	  P^1_{\bar k}\( (Hartshorne~II, Example~7.1.3, the construction of \)\mathbb
137	  P^n\( as a \)\mathrm{Proj}\(). When \)\mathcal L$ is non-trivial the present
138	  lemma's signature is reduced to the Mathlib API by working on the open
139	  cover \(X = D(s_0) \cup D(s_1)\), where on each chart the sections become
140	  honest global functions (\(s_1 / s_0\) on \(D(s_0)\), \(s_0 / s_1\) on \(D(s_1)\),
141	  agreeing on the overlap). The chart-glue is performed by the
142	  project-bespoke wrapper
143	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphismToP1OfGlobalSections}, whose body
144	  in \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RationalCurveIso.lean} chains
145	  \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} with the chart-restriction-and-glue
146	  pattern of \texttt{Scheme.Cover.glueMorphisms}.
147	  Uniqueness follows from the standard separation argument for morphisms to
148	  a separated scheme: two morphisms \(\varphi, \psi \colon X \to \mathbb P^1\)
149	  with \(\varphi^* x_i = \psi^* x_i\) for \(i = 0, 1\) agree on the basic-open
150	  charts \(D_+(x_0), D_+(x_1)\) (where they pull back the affine coordinates to
151	  the same rational function), and these charts cover \(\mathbb P^1\), so the
152	  two morphisms agree globally by gluing.
153	\end{proof}
154	\section{Degree of a morphism to \(\mathbb P^1\) via its pole divisor}
155	% NOTE (iter-190 plan-phase Pin 2 corrective; supersedes iter-182 narrative).
156	% `Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor \varphi` is the Weil divisor \(\varphi^*[\infty]\),
157	% defined as `positivePart (principal (\varphi^{\#} t_\infty))` in Lean.
158	% The supporting `Scheme.WeilDivisor.positivePart` def lives in
159	% `RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex` (\ref{def:WeilDivisor_positivePart}); the
160	% Hartshorne~II.6.9 identity that powers the existential degree statement
161	% lives there as \ref{lem:degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank}.
162	\begin{lemma}
163	
164	
165	\leanok
166	[Degree of a non-constant morphism to \(\mathbb P^1\) via the preimage of \(\infty\)]
167	  \label{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor}
168	  \uses{def:WeilDivisor_positivePart,
169	        lem:degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank, def:principal_divisor,
170	        def:divisor_degree, thm:principal_deg_zero}
171	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphism_degree_via_pole_divisor}
172	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV \S2, opening paragraph of "Hurwitz's Theorem"
173	  % (definition of the degree of a finite morphism of curves) (read from
174	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 316, doc p.~299).
175	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Recall that the \emph{degree} of a finite morphism
176	  % \(f: X \to Y\) of curves is defined to be the degree \([K(X):K(Y)]\) of the
177	  % extension of function fields (II, \S 6)."
178	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.2, p.~299, opening of \S2.}
179	  Let \(\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) be a non-constant morphism
180	  from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\). Then
181	  \(\varphi\) is finite, its function-field extension
182	  \(\bar k(\mathbb P^1) \hookrightarrow K(C)\) (the canonical
183	  \(\varphi\)-induced map, supplied as an
184	  \texttt{[Algebra (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).functionField C.functionField]}
185	  instance argument on the Lean side) is finite, and there exists a Weil
186	  divisor \(D \in \Div(C)\) such that
187	  \[
188	    D \;=\; \varphi^*[\infty]
189	    \quad\text{and}\quad
190	    \deg(D) \;=\; [\,K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)\,].
191	  \]
192	  Equivalently, the morphism \(\varphi\) satisfies
193	  \[
194	    [\,K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)\,] \;=\;
195	    \deg\!\left(\,\sum_{P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)}
196	                  \ord_P(\varphi^* t_\infty) \cdot [P]\,\right),
197	  \]
198	  where \(t_\infty \in K(\mathbb P^1)\) is a local parameter at \(\infty\)
199	  (equivalently \(t_\infty = 1/u\) for the standard affine coordinate \(u\) on
200	  \(\mathbb P^1 \setminus \{\infty\}\)); and for any closed point
201	  \(Q \in \mathbb P^1\) the same identification gives
202	  \(\deg(\varphi^*[Q]) = [K(C):\bar k(\mathbb P^1)]\).
203	  \emph{Convention on the pole divisor.} The Weil divisor
204	  \(\varphi^*[\infty]\) is exposed in Lean as
205	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor \(\varphi\)}, a
206	  noncomputable definition introduced (as a typed \texttt{sorry}) at the
207	  iter-182 plan-phase signature strengthening of this lemma. The body of
208	  \texttt{poleDivisor} is queued for iter-183+ closure via the standard
209	  affine-chart construction (\texttt{Ideal.sum\_ramification\_inertia}
210	  in Mathlib, applied at each closed point \(P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)\)
211	  to read off the local ramification multiplicity
212	  \(\ord_P(\varphi^* t_\infty)\) from the maximal ideal at \(\infty\) in
213	  \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\)). On the Lean side, the existential output of
214	  \texttt{morphism\_degree\_via\_pole\_divisor} packages
215	  \(D = \texttt{Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor}\;\varphi\) together with
216	  \(\deg(D) = \operatorname{Module.finrank}_{K(\mathbb P^1)} K(C)\),
217	  mirroring the OCofP pattern in which a typed-sorry def
218	  (\texttt{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField}) is introduced
219	  upstream of its consumer lemma.
220	\end{lemma}
221	% NOTE (iter-190 plan-phase, supersedes iter-189 review NOTE):
222	% Pin 2 corrective COMMITTED — Option (a). `Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor` is
223	% being refactored to return the *positive part* of the principal divisor
224	% of `\varphi^{\#} t_\infty`, i.e. `(div(\varphi^{\#} t_\infty))_0
225	% = \varphi^*[\infty]`. The supporting Weil-divisor `positivePart`
226	% definition lives in `RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex` as
227	% `def:WeilDivisor_positivePart`; the substantive Hartshorne~II.6.9
228	% identity that powers `lem:degree_via_pole_divisor` is now pinned as
229	% `lem:degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank` in `WeilDivisor.tex`.
230	% With these substrates in place, `lem:degree_via_pole_divisor` is
231	% mathematically correct as stated: the body's existential witness `D`
232	% is `Hom.poleDivisor \varphi = positivePart (principal (\varphi^{\#}
233	% t_\infty))`, and the degree identity is a one-line corollary of
234	% `lem:degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank`. iter-190 prover lane
235	% (Lane I) implements the refactor:
236	%   1. New def `Scheme.WeilDivisor.positivePart` in `WeilDivisor.lean`
237	%      (~10-15 LOC; lattice `D \sqcup 0`).
238	%   2. New lemma
239	%      `Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank` as a
240	%      named typed-sorry pin in `WeilDivisor.lean` (~5 LOC signature; body
241	%      ~50-80 LOC via affine-chart `Ideal.sum_ramification_inertia` route
242	%      owed iter-191+).
243	%   3. Refactor `Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor` body in `RationalCurveIso.lean`
244	%      from `principal (algebraMap _ _ t.val) halg` to
245	%      `positivePart (principal (algebraMap _ _ t.val) halg)` (~5 LOC).
246	%   4. Close `Hom.poleDivisor_degree_eq_finrank` body via a 1-line
247	%      rewrite consuming (2) (~3 LOC).
248	% Net iter-190 Lane I: 1 axiom-clean closure (Pin 2 body, modulo named
249	% typed sorry on (2)); 1 NEW named typed-sorry pin
250	% (`degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank`) which is itself an
251	% iter-191+ Hartshorne II.6.9 substrate target. See iter-190 sidecar
252	% `iter/iter-190/plan.md` for the full rationale.
253	\begin{proof}
254	  \uses{def:WeilDivisor_positivePart, lem:degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank, def:principal_divisor, def:divisor_degree, thm:principal_deg_zero}
255	  \leanok
256	  A non-constant morphism between smooth proper curves over \(\bar k\) is
257	  automatically finite. Indeed, the image of \(\varphi\) is a closed
258	  irreducible subset of \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) that is not a point (else
259	  \(\varphi\) would be constant) and not empty, so it is all of $\mathbb
260	  P^1_{\bar k}\( (the only proper irreducible closed subset of \)\mathbb
261	  P^1_{\bar k}\( is a point); hence \)\varphi$ is surjective. A surjective
262	  morphism between smooth proper irreducible curves of equal dimension is
263	  finite (proper + quasi-finite, where quasi-finiteness comes from the
264	  fact that \(\varphi\) has zero-dimensional fibres: a positive-dimensional
265	  fibre would force a one-dimensional irreducible component in the fibre,
266	  contradicting irreducibility of \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) and \(C\) at
267	  generic points). Hence the function-field extension
268	  \(\bar k(\mathbb P^1) = \varphi^\# K(\mathbb P^1) \hookrightarrow K(C)\)
269	  is finite.
270	  The identification \(\deg(\varphi^*[Q]) = [K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)]\) for
271	  any closed point \(Q \in \mathbb P^1\) is the multiplicativity of the
272	  degree under finite pullback (Hartshorne~II.6.9): for a finite morphism
273	  \(\varphi \colon C \to C'\) of smooth proper curves and any divisor $D \in
274	  \Div(C')\(, \)\deg(\varphi^* D) = \deg(\varphi) \cdot \deg(D)$. Specialising
275	  to \(D = [Q]\) (a divisor of degree \(1\) since \(\kappa(Q) = \bar k\)) gives
276	  \(\deg(\varphi^*[Q]) = \deg(\varphi)\). Equivalently, the degree of
277	  \(\varphi\) equals the number of preimages of any closed point of $\mathbb
278	  P^1\(, counted with the ramification multiplicities \)\ord_P(\varphi^*
279	  t_\infty)\( at the preimage points \)P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)$.
280	  \emph{Sub-build note.} The lemma threads two auxiliary inputs:
281	  ``non-constant morphism between smooth proper curves is finite'' (the
282	  surjective-and-proper-and-quasi-finite argument above; Hartshorne~II.6.8)
283	  and ``multiplicativity of degree under finite pullback'' (Hartshorne~II.6.9
284	  and the multiplicativity of \(\deg\) under composition of \(\div\) and
285	  \(\deg\); the closely related ``\(\mathrm{div}(f)\) has degree zero'' specialisation
286	  is \texttt{thm:principal_deg_zero} of \texttt{RR.1}). Both are separable
287	  sub-lemmas inside \texttt{RR.4}'s Lean file
288	  (\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RationalCurveIso.lean}) and are
289	  slotted for closure in a follow-up iteration after the
290	  \texttt{morphism\_degree\_via\_pole\_divisor} skeleton lands.
291	\end{proof}
292	\subsection{The pole divisor \(\varphi^*[\infty]\) and its degree (Lean substrate)}
293	% The four blocks of this subsection give 1-to-1 blueprint coverage for the
294	% Lean helper declarations underlying `lem:degree_via_pole_divisor` above
295	% (the local parameter at \(\infty\), its uniformiser property, the pole
296	% divisor itself, and the function-field-degree identity). They are
297	% project-internal plumbing; the external grounding is the Hartshorne IV.2 /
298	% II.6.9 material already cited on `lem:degree_via_pole_divisor`, so no
299	% further verbatim citation is attached here.
300	\begin{definition}[Local parameter at \(\infty \in \mathbb P^1\)]
301	  \label{def:localParameterAtInfty}
302	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.localParameterAtInfty}
303	  \uses{def:functionFieldIso}
304	  Let \(\bar k\) be a field and present the projective line as
305	  \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k} = \operatorname{Proj} \bar k[X_0, X_1]\). The
306	  \emph{local parameter at \(\infty\)} is the rational function
307	  \[
308	    t_\infty \;:=\; X_0 / X_1 \;\in\; K(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}),
309	  \]
310	  the degree-\(0\) ratio coordinate of the homogeneous localisation
311	  \(\bigl(\bar k[X_0, X_1]_{(X_1)}\bigr)_0 = \mathcal O\!\bigl(D_+(X_1)\bigr)\),
312	  transported into the function field \(K(\mathbb P^1)\) along the germ map
313	  on the affine chart \(D_+(X_1)\). On the Lean side it is packaged as a
314	  \emph{nonzero} element of \(K(\mathbb P^1)\): the witness \(t_\infty \neq 0\)
315	  comes from the injectivity of the chart isomorphism
316	  \(\mathcal O\!\bigl(D_+(X_1)\bigr) \cong \bar k[X_0/X_1]\) (a
317	  \(\operatorname{Proj}\)-chart presentation) composed with the injective
318	  germ-to-function-field map of the integral scheme \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\),
319	  applied to the nonzero ratio element \(X_0 / X_1\).
320	\end{definition}
321	\begin{proof}
322	  Proved directly in Lean.
323	\end{proof}
324	\begin{lemma}[\(t_\infty\) is a uniformiser at \([\infty]\)]
325	  \label{lem:localParameterAtInfty_uniformiser_witness}
326	  \uses{def:localParameterAtInfty}
327	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.localParameterAtInfty_uniformiser_witness}
328	  There is a unique prime divisor \(Y_0 \in \Div(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k})\) — the
329	  point at infinity \([\infty]\) — at which the local parameter
330	  \(t_\infty = X_0 / X_1\) of \cref{def:localParameterAtInfty} has positive
331	  order, and there \(\ord_{Y_0}(t_\infty) = 1\). Equivalently, the zero divisor
332	  \((t_\infty)_0 = [\infty]\) of \(t_\infty\) on \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) has
333	  degree \(1\) and is supported at the single prime divisor \([\infty]\).
334	\end{lemma}
335	\begin{proof}
336	  \uses{def:localParameterAtInfty}
337	  \emph{Honest sketch (the Lean body is an open hole).} The witness \(Y_0\) is
338	  the closed point at infinity, image under \(\operatorname{Proj}.\mathrm{aw\!ay\iota}\)
339	  at \(X_1\) of the maximal ideal generated by \(X_0/X_1\) in the affine chart
340	  \(\operatorname{Spec} \bar k[X_0/X_1]\). On that chart the stalk at \(Y_0\) is
341	  the discrete valuation ring \(\bar k[X_0/X_1]_{(X_0/X_1)}\) with uniformiser
342	  \(X_0/X_1 = t_\infty\), so \(\ord_{Y_0}(t_\infty) = 1\); and on the
343	  complementary chart \(D_+(X_0)\) the function \(t_\infty\) is a unit, whence
344	  no other prime divisor carries positive order. Formalising this requires the
345	  not-yet-available stalk-of-\(\operatorname{Proj}\)-chart-is-a-DVR bridge and
346	  the order-via-uniformiser identity (the same codimension-one regularity
347	  substrate that \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\)'s
348	  \texttt{IsRegularInCodimensionOne} instance is gated on); pending that, the
349	  Lean proof is left as an explicit hole.
350	\end{proof}
351	\begin{definition}[Pole divisor of \(\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1\)]
352	  \label{def:hom_poleDivisor}
353	  \uses{def:localParameterAtInfty, def:WeilDivisor_positivePart, def:principal_divisor}
354	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor}
355	  Let \(C / \bar k\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve, regular
356	  in codimension one, and let \(\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) be a
357	  morphism, with \(K(C)\) carrying the canonical \(\varphi\)-induced
358	  \(K(\mathbb P^1)\)-algebra structure. The \emph{pole divisor} of \(\varphi\)
359	  is the Weil divisor
360	  \[
361	    \varphi^*[\infty]
362	    \;:=\;
363	    \bigl(\operatorname{div}(\varphi^\# t_\infty)\bigr)_0
364	    \;=\;
365	    \operatorname{positivePart}\!\bigl(\operatorname{div}(\varphi^\# t_\infty)\bigr)
366	    \;\in\; \Div(C),
367	  \]
368	  the positive part of the principal divisor on \(C\) of the pulled-back local
369	  parameter \(\varphi^\# t_\infty = \operatorname{algebraMap}_{K(\mathbb P^1) \to K(C)}(t_\infty)\),
370	  where \(t_\infty\) is the local parameter of \cref{def:localParameterAtInfty}.
371	  The pullback is nonzero because the algebra map \(K(\mathbb P^1) \to K(C)\) is
372	  injective (a ring homomorphism out of a field), so \(t_\infty \neq 0\) gives
373	  \(\varphi^\# t_\infty \neq 0\) and the principal divisor is well defined. On a
374	  complete nonsingular curve the zeros of \(\varphi^\# t_\infty\) are exactly the
375	  preimages of \(\infty\), so the positive part recovers \(\varphi^*[\infty]\) on
376	  the nose.
377	\end{definition}
378	\begin{proof}
379	  Proved directly in Lean.
380	\end{proof}
381	\begin{theorem}[Degree of the pole divisor equals the function-field degree]
382	  \label{thm:poleDivisor_degree_eq_finrank}
383	  \uses{def:hom_poleDivisor, lem:localParameterAtInfty_uniformiser_witness,
384	        lem:degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank, def:divisor_degree}
385	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor_degree_eq_finrank}
386	  For a non-constant morphism \(\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) of
387	  smooth proper geometrically irreducible curves over \(\bar k\) (with \(K(C)\)
388	  finite over \(K(\mathbb P^1)\) via the \(\varphi\)-induced algebra structure),
389	  the degree of the pole divisor equals the function-field extension degree:
390	  \[
391	    \deg\!\bigl(\varphi^*[\infty]\bigr)
392	    \;=\;
393	    [\,K(C) : K(\mathbb P^1)\,]
394	    \;=\;
395	    \operatorname{finrank}_{K(\mathbb P^1)} K(C).
396	  \]
397	  This is the precise form of the degree identity packaged existentially by
398	  \cref{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor}.
399	\end{theorem}
400	\begin{proof}
401	  \uses{def:hom_poleDivisor, lem:localParameterAtInfty_uniformiser_witness,
402	        lem:degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank}
403	  Proved directly in Lean: unfolding \(\varphi^*[\infty] =
404	  \operatorname{positivePart}(\operatorname{div}(\varphi^\# t_\infty))\)
405	  reduces the claim to the Hartshorne~II.6.9 degree identity
406	  \cref{lem:degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank} of \texttt{RR.1},
407	  specialised at the local parameter \(t = t_\infty\), whose uniformiser
408	  hypothesis is supplied by \cref{lem:localParameterAtInfty_uniformiser_witness}.
409	\end{proof}

411	% NOTE (iter-181 Lane I signature refinement; see analogies/ratcurveiso-pin3.md
412	% Decision 2, verdict DIVERGE_INTENTIONALLY). The iter-177 file-skeleton

423	% NOTE (iter-194 reviewer; refreshed iter-196): The Lean

425	% 16 LOC of `Subalgebra.bot_eq_top_of_finrank_eq_one +

441	\begin{lemma}
442	
443	
444	\leanok
445	[Degree-\(1\) morphism between smooth proper curves is an isomorphism]
446	  \label{lem:degree_one_morphism_iso}
447	  \uses{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor, def:functionFieldIso}
448	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.iso_of_degree_one}
449	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], I \S6 Corollary~6.12 (function-field-to-curve
450	  % equivalence of categories) (read from
451	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 62, doc p.~45)
452	  % + [Hartshorne], IV \S2 opening paragraph (degree of a finite morphism
453	  % of curves is the function-field extension degree) (read from
454	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 316, doc p.~299).
455	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Corollary 6.12. \emph{The following three categories are
456	  % equivalent:}
457	  % (i) \emph{nonsingular projective curves, and dominant morphisms;}
458	  % (ii) \emph{quasi-projective curves, and dominant rational maps;}
459	  % (iii) \emph{function fields of dimension 1 over \(k\), and \(k\)-homomorphisms.}"
460	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Recall that the \emph{degree} of a finite morphism
461	  % \(f: X \to Y\) of curves is defined to be the degree \([K(X):K(Y)]\) of the
462	  % extension of function fields (II, \S 6)."
463	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, I.6 Corollary~6.12 (p.~45) + IV.2 opening
464	  (p.~299).}
465	  Let \(C, C'\) be smooth proper geometrically irreducible curves over an
466	  algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), and let \(\varphi \colon C \to C'\) be
467	  a non-constant morphism whose induced function-field extension is of
468	  degree one,
469	  \[
470	    [\,K(C) : K(C')\,] \;=\; 1,
471	  \]
472	  formally
473	  \(\operatorname{Module.finrank}_{K(C')} K(C) = 1\) in the Lean signature.
474	  Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes.
475	  \emph{Convention on the function-field map.} At each call-site of this
476	  lemma, the \(K(C')\)-algebra structure on \(K(C)\) used to interpret the
477	  degree \([K(C) : K(C')]\) is the canonical \(\varphi\)-induced
478	  function-field map, i.e.\ the composite
479	  \[
480	    K(C') \;\xrightarrow{\;\text{germ}\;}\;
481	    \mathcal O_{C', \varphi(\xi_C)}
482	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\varphi^\#\;}\;
483	    \mathcal O_{C, \xi_C}
484	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\text{frac.}\,\text{lift}\;}\; K(C)
485	  \]
486	  of the germ at the image of the generic point \(\xi_C \in C\) with the
487	  stalk-map of \(\varphi\) at \(\xi_C\) and the fraction-field lift at
488	  \(\xi_C\) (per \texttt{analogies/ratcurveiso-pin3.md}, Decision~2). On
489	  the Lean side this canonical instance is carried as a
490	  \texttt{[Algebra C'.functionField C.functionField]} instance argument
491	  to \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.iso\_of\_degree\_one}; consumers
492	  supply it explicitly at each call-site.
493	\end{lemma}
494	% NOTE (iter-189 review): Pin 3 Step 1 (function-field iso K(C') ≃+* K(C)
495	% from `Module.finrank = 1`) closed axiom-clean inline iter-189 (~10 LOC via
496	% `Subalgebra.bot_eq_top_of_finrank_eq_one` + `Algebra.surjective_algebraMap_iff`
497	% + `RingHom.injective` + `RingEquiv.ofBijective`). Step 2 (scheme-level lift
498	% via `Scheme.Hom.toNormalization`) remains a typed sorry in the body, with 4
499	% sub-obligations (a)-(d) documented inline at the sorry. Iter-190+ closure
500	% budget ~80-150 LOC per `analogies/ratcurveiso-pin3.md`.
501	\begin{proof}
502	
503	
504	
505	\leanok
506	  The function-field hypothesis \([K(C) : K(C')] = 1\) states that the
507	  canonical \(\varphi\)-induced map \(\varphi^\# \colon K(C') \to K(C)\) is a
508	  finite extension of degree one; since \(K(C')\) is a field and the
509	  extension has dimension one as a \(K(C')\)-vector space, \(\varphi^\#\) is
510	  an isomorphism of function fields \(K(C') \cong K(C)\). (Equivalently, by
511	  the IV.2-opening identification \(\deg(\varphi) = [K(C):K(C')]\), this is
512	  the statement \(\deg(\varphi) = 1\); the morphism \(\varphi\) is finite in
513	  this case by the standard argument that a non-constant morphism of
514	  smooth proper curves is finite, identical to the
515	  \texttt{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor} preamble.)
516	  By Hartshorne's Corollary~I.6.12, the assignment \(C \mapsto \bar k(C)\) is
517	  an equivalence of categories between smooth projective curves over $\bar
518	  k$ (with dominant morphisms) and function fields of transcendence degree
519	  one over \(\bar k\) (with \(\bar k\)-homomorphisms). Under this equivalence,
520	  the isomorphism \(\bar k(C') \cong \bar k(C)\) produced by the previous
521	  paragraph lifts to a unique isomorphism of smooth proper curves $C \to
522	  C'\(, and this isomorphism agrees with \)\varphi$ on the generic point (by
523	  construction: it is the inverse-image of \(\varphi^\#\) under the
524	  equivalence). The two morphisms \(C \to C'\) that agree on the generic
525	  point of the integral scheme \(C\) agree everywhere (a morphism out of an
526	  integral scheme to a separated scheme is determined by its restriction
527	  to the generic point, Hartshorne~II Ex.~4.2). Hence \(\varphi\) is itself
528	  the lift, and is therefore an isomorphism.
529	  \emph{Alternative direct argument.} Without invoking the
530	  equivalence-of-categories framing, the same conclusion is obtained
531	  scheme-theoretically: a finite degree-\(1\) morphism $\varphi \colon C \to
532	  C'$ between integral normal noetherian schemes is an isomorphism.
533	  Indeed, the direct image \(\varphi_* \mathcal O_C\) is a coherent
534	  \(\mathcal O_{C'}\)-module that is generically of rank $\deg(\varphi) =
535	  1\(, and an inclusion \)\mathcal O_{C'} \hookrightarrow \varphi_*
536	  \mathcal O_C\( of coherent rank-\)1$ sheaves whose generic ranks agree
537	  is an isomorphism over the locus where the quotient
538	  \(\varphi_* \mathcal O_C / \mathcal O_{C'}\) has trivial stalk. The
539	  quotient is a torsion coherent \(\mathcal O_{C'}\)-module of generic rank
540	  \(\deg(\varphi) - 1 = 0\), hence is itself torsion-free (a
541	  finitely-generated torsion module over a Dedekind domain of generic
542	  rank zero is zero), so vanishes. Therefore $\varphi_* \mathcal O_C =
543	  \mathcal O_{C'}\( and \)\varphi$ is the structure morphism of an
544	  isomorphism. Cf.\ Stacks tag~\textbf{0AVX} for the categorical version.
545	  \emph{Sub-build note.} The Lean signature
546	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.iso\_of\_degree\_one} consumes a
547	  non-constant morphism \(\varphi \colon C \to C'\) together with a
548	  \texttt{[Algebra C'.functionField C.functionField]} instance argument
549	  (the canonical \(\varphi\)-induced function-field map) and the
550	  function-field-extension-degree-one hypothesis
551	  \texttt{Module.finrank C'.functionField C.functionField = 1}, and
552	  produces an isomorphism \(C \cong C'\) in the slice category
553	  \(\mathtt{Over (Spec (.of\ kbar))}\). The proof body threads the Mathlib
554	  API \texttt{IsIntegralHom.iso\_of\_isUnit} (or the equivalent
555	  \texttt{IsAffineHom + Finite + RankOne} chain) and, where needed, the
556	  project-bespoke ``finite-morphism-of-degree-one is an isomorphism''
557	  helper. The Hartshorne~I.6.12 equivalence-of-categories framing is the
558	  intended high-level proof structure; the scheme-theoretic argument
559	  above is the spelled-out form for the Lean body.
560	\end{proof}
561	\subsection{The normalization factorisation helpers (Lean substrate)}
562	% The five blocks of this subsection give 1-to-1 blueprint coverage for the
563	% Lean helper declarations into which the body of `lem:degree_one_morphism_iso`
564	% (`iso_of_degree_one`) decomposes: the bijectivity of the function-field
565	% algebra map under finrank one, the induced function-field iso, local
566	% quasi-finiteness of the underlying morphism, and the two normalization
567	% factors. They are project-internal plumbing whose external grounding is the
568	% Hartshorne I.6.12 / Stacks 0AVX material already cited on
569	% `lem:degree_one_morphism_iso`.
570	\begin{lemma}[Degree-one algebra map over a field is bijective]
571	  \label{lem:algebraMap_bijective_of_finrank_one}
572	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.algebraMap_bijective_of_finrank_one}
573	  Let \(K\) be a field and \(L\) a \(K\)-algebra with
574	  \(\operatorname{finrank}_K L = 1\). Then the structure map
575	  \(\operatorname{algebraMap} \colon K \to L\) is bijective.
576	\end{lemma}
577	\begin{proof}
578	  Proved directly in Lean. A subalgebra of dimension one over a field is the
579	  whole algebra, i.e. \(\bot = \top\) as \(K\)-subalgebras of \(L\); over a field
580	  this is equivalent to the structure map being bijective.
581	\end{proof}
582	\begin{definition}[Function-field isomorphism from a degree-one extension]
583	  \label{def:phi_left_functionField_algEquiv_of_finrank_one}
584	  \uses{lem:algebraMap_bijective_of_finrank_one}
585	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.phi_left_functionField_algEquiv_of_finrank_one}
586	  For integral schemes \(C, C'\) over \(\bar k\) with \(K(C)\) a
587	  \(K(C')\)-algebra of dimension one
588	  (\(\operatorname{finrank}_{K(C')} K(C) = 1\)), the structure map is the ring
589	  isomorphism
590	  \[
591	    K(C') \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; K(C)
592	  \]
593	  obtained from the bijectivity of \cref{lem:algebraMap_bijective_of_finrank_one}.
594	\end{definition}
595	\begin{proof}
596	  Proved directly in Lean.
597	\end{proof}
598	\begin{lemma}[Local quasi-finiteness of a degree-one morphism]
599	  \label{lem:phi_left_locallyQuasiFinite_of_finrank_one}
600	  \uses{def:phi_left_functionField_algEquiv_of_finrank_one}
601	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.phi_left_locallyQuasiFinite_of_finrank_one}
602	  Let \(C, C'\) be smooth proper geometrically irreducible curves over an
603	  algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) and \(\varphi \colon C \to C'\) a
604	  morphism whose induced function-field extension has degree one
605	  (\(\operatorname{finrank}_{K(C')} K(C) = 1\)). Then the underlying scheme
606	  morphism \(\varphi\) is locally quasi-finite. Combined with properness this
607	  upgrades to finiteness (and hence to an integral, affine morphism).
608	\end{lemma}
609	\begin{proof}
610	  \uses{def:phi_left_functionField_algEquiv_of_finrank_one}
611	  \emph{Honest sketch (the Lean body is an open hole).} By properness of
612	  \(\varphi\) (read off from the triangle over \(\operatorname{Spec} \bar k\))
613	  it suffices, via the reduction to fibre-set finiteness, to show every fibre
614	  \(\varphi^{-1}(x)\) is finite. At the generic point of \(C'\) the fibre is
615	  \(\operatorname{Spec} K(C) \to \operatorname{Spec} K(C')\), a single point
616	  because the function-field map is the isomorphism of
617	  \cref{def:phi_left_functionField_algEquiv_of_finrank_one}. At a closed point
618	  the fibre is zero-dimensional because both curves are smooth of relative
619	  dimension one over \(\bar k\). The closed-point case rests on the
620	  ``smooth of relative dimension one \(\Rightarrow\) zero-dimensional fibre''
621	  statement, which is not yet available in the project; pending it, the Lean
622	  proof is left as an explicit hole.
623	\end{proof}
624	\begin{lemma}[Normalization-target factor is an isomorphism]
625	  \label{lem:phi_left_toNormalization_isIso_of_isIntegralHom}
626	  \uses{lem:phi_left_locallyQuasiFinite_of_finrank_one}
627	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.phi_left_toNormalization_isIso_of_isIntegralHom}
628	  For a quasi-compact, quasi-separated, integral morphism
629	  \(\varphi \colon C \to C'\) of schemes, the dominant factor of the
630	  normalization factorisation,
631	  \(\varphi.\mathrm{toNormalization} \colon C \to \widetilde{C'}_\varphi\), is an
632	  isomorphism. (The integrality hypothesis is the output of
633	  \cref{lem:phi_left_locallyQuasiFinite_of_finrank_one} together with
634	  properness.)
635	\end{lemma}
636	\begin{proof}
637	  \uses{lem:phi_left_locallyQuasiFinite_of_finrank_one}
638	  Proved directly in Lean: this is the Mathlib instance attaching
639	  \texttt{IsIso} to \(\varphi.\mathrm{toNormalization}\) for an integral
640	  quasi-compact quasi-separated morphism (the dominant half of the
641	  Hartshorne~I.6.12 / Stacks~\textbf{0AVX} factorisation).
642	\end{proof}
643	\begin{lemma}[Normalization-source factor is an isomorphism over a smooth target]
644	  \label{lem:phi_left_fromNormalization_isIso_of_smoothProper_finrank_one}
645	  \uses{def:phi_left_functionField_algEquiv_of_finrank_one}
646	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.phi_left_fromNormalization_isIso_of_smoothProper_finrank_one}
647	  Let \(C'\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(\bar k\),
648	  let \(C\) be integral, and let \(\varphi \colon C \to C'\) be a quasi-compact
649	  quasi-separated morphism whose induced function-field extension has degree one
650	  (\(\operatorname{finrank}_{K(C')} K(C) = 1\)). Then the integral factor of the
651	  normalization factorisation,
652	  \(\varphi.\mathrm{fromNormalization} \colon \widetilde{C'}_\varphi \to C'\), is
653	  an isomorphism.
654	\end{lemma}
655	\begin{proof}
656	  \uses{def:phi_left_functionField_algEquiv_of_finrank_one}
657	  \emph{Honest sketch (the Lean body is an open hole).} A smooth proper curve
658	  over \(\bar k\) is regular, hence normal, so its affine sections are integrally
659	  closed (Dedekind) domains. Under the degree-one hypothesis the function-field
660	  iso of \cref{def:phi_left_functionField_algEquiv_of_finrank_one} identifies
661	  \(K(C)\) with \(K(C')\), so the integral closure of each \(\Gamma(C', U)\)
662	  inside \(\Gamma(C, \varphi^{-1}U)\) collapses to \(\Gamma(C', U)\) itself; thus
663	  \(\varphi.\mathrm{fromNormalization}\) is an isomorphism. Formalising this needs
664	  a scheme-level normality substrate (``smooth curve over a field has Dedekind
665	  sections / is a normal scheme'') not yet present in the project; pending it,
666	  the Lean proof is left as an explicit hole.
667	\end{proof}
668	\section{Headline: genus-\(0\) curve over \(\bar k\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1\)}
669	\begin{theorem}
670	
671	\leanok
672	[Genus-\(0\) curve over \(\bar k\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1\)]
673	  \label{thm:genus_zero_curve_iso_p1}
674	  \uses{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero, cor:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_exists_nonconstant_genusZero,
675	        lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections, lem:degree_via_pole_divisor,
676	        lem:degree_one_morphism_iso, def:genus}
677	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
678	  % already know that \(p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0\) (I, Ex. 7.2), so conversely
679	  % suppose given a curve \(X\) of genus 0. Let \(P, Q\) be two distinct
680	  % points of \(X\) and apply Riemann-Roch to the divisor \(D = P - Q\).
681	  % Since \(\deg(K - D) = -2\), using (1.3.3) above, we have \(l(K - D) = 0\),
682	  % and so we find \(l(D) = 1\). But \(D\) is a divisor of degree 0, so by
683	  % (1.2) we have \(D \sim 0\), in other words \(P \sim Q\). But this implies
684	  % \(X\) is rational (II, 6.10.1)."
685	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Example~1.3.5, p.~297.}
686	  Let \(\bar k\) be an algebraically closed field, and let \(C\) be a smooth
687	  proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\).
688	  Then there exists an isomorphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes
689	  \[
690	    \varphi \colon C \stackrel{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}.
691	  \]
692	  Concretely, the Lean signature
693	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1} (the existing pinned
694	  target at \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean:290}) is
695	  closed by the proof below; the existing interface block
696	  \texttt{chap:RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso} of
697	  \texttt{chap:avr_for_rr} is its consumer-side restatement,
698	  pinning the same Lean declaration.
699	\end{theorem}
700	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the verbatim Example IV.1.3.5 text in the
701	% statement block above (Hartshorne states the classification and its
702	% proof inside the single Example; the proof is the second half beginning
703	% "We already know that \(p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0\) \ldots"). The present
704	% proof body restates Hartshorne's argument in the project's notation
705	% (linear-system construction \(C \to \mathbb P^1\) via $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C
706	% ([P]))\(, degree-\)1$ identification, isomorphism).
707	\begin{proof}
708	  Over the algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) the smooth proper
709	  geometrically irreducible curve \(C\) has closed points, all of which are
710	  \(\bar k\)-rational (the residue field of a closed point of a curve over
711	  \(\bar k\) is a finite extension of \(\bar k\), hence is \(\bar k\) itself by
712	  algebraic closedness). Fix one such \(\bar k\)-rational point \(P \in C\);
713	  this is the marked point used to build the linear system below.
714	  \emph{Step 1: non-constant function \(f \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))\).}
715	  By Hartshorne's Riemann--Roch in genus zero
716	  (\texttt{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero} of \texttt{RR.2}) applied to the
717	  effective divisor \([P]\) of degree \(1\), we obtain
718	  \(\ell([P]) = \deg([P]) + 1 = 2\). The constant section
719	  \(1 \in \bar k \cdot 1 \subset H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))\) accounts for a
720	  one-dimensional \(\bar k\)-subspace; the two-dimensional quotient supplies
721	  a non-constant section $f \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P])) \setminus
722	  \bar k \cdot 1$. This is the corollary
723	  \texttt{cor:nonconstant\_function\_genus\_zero} of \texttt{RR.3} (sibling
724	  chapter, \texttt{RiemannRoch\_OCofP.tex}).
725	  \emph{Step 2: morphism \(C \to \mathbb P^1\) via the linear system \((1, f)\).}
726	  The pair \((1, f) \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))^2\) generates the
727	  invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) at every point of \(C\): at points
728	  \(Q \in C \setminus \{P\}\) the section \(1\) does not vanish in
729	  \(\mathcal O_C([P])_Q \cong \mathcal O_{C, Q}\), and at the marked point
730	  \(P\) the section \(f\) does not vanish in \(\mathcal O_C([P])_P\) (because \(f\)
731	  has a pole of order exactly \(1\) at \(P\) in the rational-function
732	  picture: \(f \in K(C) \setminus \bar k\) with \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty = [P]\)).
733	  Therefore by \texttt{lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections} there
734	  exists a unique morphism
735	  \[
736	    \varphi \;:=\; \varphi_{(1, f)} \colon C \longrightarrow \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}
737	  \]
738	  with \(\varphi^* x_0 = 1\) and \(\varphi^* x_1 = f\) (so the pullback of
739	  the standard ratio coordinate \(u = x_1 / x_0\) on $\mathbb P^1 \setminus
740	  \{\infty\}\( is \)f$, and the pullback of the inverse coordinate
741	  \(t_\infty = x_0 / x_1 = 1 / f\) is the local parameter \(1/f\) at \(P\)).
742	  \emph{Step 3: \(\deg(\varphi) = 1\) via the pole divisor of \(f\).} The
743	  morphism \(\varphi\) is non-constant (since \(f\) is non-constant as a
744	  rational function on \(C\), the pullback \(\varphi^* u = f\) takes
745	  infinitely many values, ruling out a constant \(\varphi\)). By
746	  \texttt{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor} applied to \(\varphi\),
747	  \[
748	    \deg(\varphi)
749	    \;=\;
750	    \deg\!\left(\,\sum_{Q \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)}
751	                  \ord_Q(\varphi^* t_\infty) \cdot [Q]\,\right).
752	  \]
753	  We compute the pole divisor of \(f\) as a rational function on \(C\):
754	  since \(f \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))\) with \(f\) non-constant and
755	  \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) the line bundle of the effective divisor \([P]\), the
756	  rational-function \(f\) has pole divisor \((f)_\infty = [P]\) (the
757	  unique pole of order \(1\) at \(P\), no other poles; this is the standard
758	  identification of \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))\) with the rational functions
759	  \(g \in K(C)\) satisfying \(\mathrm{div}(g) + D \geq 0\), specialised to
760	  \(D = [P]\)). Hence \(\varphi^{-1}(\infty) = \{P\}\) with multiplicity~\(1\)
761	  and
762	  \[
763	    \deg(\varphi) \;=\; \deg([P]) \;=\; 1.
764	  \]
765	  \emph{Step 4: \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism.} By Hartshorne's IV.2
766	  identification \(\deg(\varphi) = [K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)]\), the
767	  degree-one conclusion of Step~3 rewrites as
768	  \(\operatorname{Module.finrank}_{\bar k(\mathbb P^1)} K(C) = 1\) (with
769	  the canonical \(\varphi\)-induced
770	  \texttt{[Algebra \,(ProjectiveLineBar kbar).functionField\,
771	   C.functionField]} instance). By
772	  \texttt{lem:degree_one_morphism_iso} applied to the non-constant
773	  morphism \(\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) (smooth proper
774	  geometrically irreducible curves over \(\bar k\) on both sides), under
775	  this function-field-extension-degree-one hypothesis, \(\varphi\) is an
776	  isomorphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes.
777	  This produces the required isomorphism \(C \cong \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\)
778	  closing the headline target
779	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}.
780	  \emph{Comparison with Hartshorne's original argument.} Hartshorne's
781	  Example~IV.1.3.5 phrases the argument in terms of two distinct points
782	  \(P, Q \in C\) and the degree-zero divisor \(D = P - Q\), deriving \(P \sim Q\)
783	  and concluding via II.6.10.1 that \(C\) is rational; combined with I.6.12
784	  this yields \(C \cong \mathbb P^1\). The present proof restates the same
785	  combinatorial content via the more directly formalisable linear-system
786	  construction: instead of going through the \(P \sim Q\) statement and
787	  invoking II.6.10.1 as a black box, we produce the explicit morphism
788	  \(\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1\) from the two sections \((1, f)\) of
789	  \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) given by Riemann--Roch and identify its degree
790	  directly. The two arguments are mathematically equivalent and have the
791	  same dependency footprint (Riemann--Roch in genus zero \(\Rightarrow\)
792	  \(\ell([P]) = 2\) \(\Rightarrow\) non-constant \(f\) \(\Rightarrow\) degree-\(1\)
793	  map to \(\mathbb P^1\) \(\Rightarrow\) iso); the linear-system phrasing maps
794	  more cleanly onto the Mathlib \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} API and
795	  the project's existing Weil-divisor/degree pipeline of \texttt{RR.1}.
796	\end{proof}
797	\begin{remark}
798	[Drop of \texttt{sorryAx} on the genus-\(0\) Albanese keystone]
799	  \label{rmk:rr4_unblocks_sorryAx_on_rigidity_genus0}
800	  \uses{thm:genus_zero_curve_iso_p1}
801	  Closure of \texttt{chap:RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso} retires the
802	  \texttt{sorryAx} propagation tagged on
803	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity\_genus0\_curve\_to\_grpScheme}
804	  (\texttt{thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} of
805	  \texttt{chap:avr_for_rr}, body landed iter-166), whose
806	  \emph{first} of two upstream \texttt{sorry} sources is the
807	  \texttt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1} target this chapter closes. The
808	  \emph{second} upstream is the helper
809	  \texttt{morphism\_P1\_to\_grpScheme\_const}'s residual sorries, tracked
810	  separately under the genus-\(0\) Albanese arm of \texttt{Jacobian.tex}.
811	\end{remark}
812	\section{Out of scope}
813	The following items are deliberately \emph{not} part of \texttt{RR.4} and
814	are sequenced elsewhere:
815	\begin{itemize}
816	  \item The general ``smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C\) of
817	    genus \(0\) over a \emph{non-closed} field \(k\) with a \(k\)-rational point
818	    \(\cong \mathbb P^1_k\)'' statement is the \(\bar k \to k\) descent of
819	    \texttt{chap:RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso}. The descent is hosted in
820	    \texttt{Jacobian.tex} via \texttt{genusZeroWitness.key}; it consumes
821	    \texttt{chap:RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso} together with Galois descent for
822	    smooth proper curves and is out of scope for \texttt{RR.4}.
823	  \item The Mathlib API \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Proj.fromOfGlobalSections}
824	    (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.ProjectiveSpectrum.Basic}) is
825	    consumed as a black box by
826	    \texttt{lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections}; its own proof inside
827	    Mathlib (the universal property of \(\mathrm{Proj}\), graded-ring
828	    presentation of \(\mathbb P^n\)) is not re-derived here.
829	  \item Multiplicativity of degree under finite pullback (Hartshorne~II.6.9)
830	    is consumed as a black box by \texttt{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor};
831	    its proof is a separable sub-lemma threaded inside
832	    \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RationalCurveIso.lean} and is
833	    \emph{not} part of \texttt{RR.4}'s blueprint statement (it lives at
834	    the same logical level as the closely related
835	    \texttt{thm:principal_deg_zero} of \texttt{RR.1}).
836	  \item The full equivalence of categories ``smooth projective curves over
837	    \(\bar k\) with dominant morphisms'' \(\cong\) ``function fields of
838	    transcendence degree~\(1\) over \(\bar k\) with \(\bar k\)-homomorphisms''
839	    (Hartshorne~I.6.12) is consumed as a high-level framing in
840	    \texttt{lem:degree_one_morphism_iso} but is \emph{not} re-formalised at
841	    the equivalence-of-categories level here. The proof body in the Lean
842	    file threads the spelled-out scheme-theoretic ``finite-degree-one
843	    morphism between integral normal schemes is an isomorphism''
844	    argument (Stacks~\textbf{0AVX}), which is the minimal-Mathlib-dependence
845	    pin compatible with the chapter's downstream needs.
846	  \item ``A smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(\bar k\)
847	    has at least two distinct \(\bar k\)-points'' (the non-empty / infinite
848	    cardinality of \(C(\bar k)\)) is consumed implicitly by the present
849	    chapter but its formal statement lives in the project's curve API
850	    (\texttt{Genus.tex} / \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian.Genus}); the chapter
851	    does not restate it.
852	  \item Cohomology of the line bundle \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) on a curve
853	    (Hartshorne~III.5 + IV.1) is fully consumed via \texttt{RR.3}; in
854	    particular the \(H^1\)-vanishing of \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) on a genus-\(0\)
855	    curve is part of \texttt{RR.3}'s payload feeding
856	    \texttt{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero} of \texttt{RR.2}, and is not
857	    re-derived here.
858	\end{itemize}

3	\chapter{The Riemann--Roch formula in genus zero (RR.2)}
4	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_RRFormula}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, IV \S1, pp.~294--297
7	% (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages
8	% 311--314). The verbatim quotes pasted in the declaration blocks below
9	% were copied character-by-character from the rendered page images of
10	% the scanned PDF (no text layer is present).

12	\section{Setup and motivation}
13	
14	This chapter is the second of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
15	that close the project's headline RR bridge
16	\[
17	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
18	  \,\colon\,
19	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
20	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
21	\]
22	The bridge consumed by
23	REF of
24	\texttt{AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex} is the Hartshorne~IV.1.3.5 chain:
25	the Weil-divisor group \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\) and the degree map $\deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C)
26	\to \mathbb Z$ (\texttt{RR.1}, REF); the
27	Riemann--Roch dimension formula in genus~\(0\)
28	\[
29	  \ell(D) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 \qquad \text{for } \deg(D) \geq 0
30	\]
31	(this chapter, \texttt{RR.2}); the global-sections identification
32	\(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) (\texttt{RR.3}); the
33	``rational curve \(\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1\)'' classification via
34	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
35	
36	% NOTE (Serre duality vs. direct \(\chi\) — iter-173 sub-phase choice).
37	% This chapter proves the RR formula via the \emph{direct \(\chi\)
38	% inductive proof} (Hartshorne IV.1.3 step ``Next, let \(D\) be any
39	% divisor\ldots'', p.~296), \emph{not} via Serre duality. The reason is
40	% strictly Mathlib-side: Mathlib (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) ships no
41	% dualizing sheaf \(\omega_X\) on a scheme and no Serre duality theorem
42	% \(H^i(X, \mathcal F) \cong H^{n-i}(X, \omega_X \otimes \mathcal F^\vee)^*\);
43	% routing through that machinery would force a multi-iter sub-build
44	% strictly heavier than the genus-\(0\) critical path requires. The
45	% direct \(\chi\) proof depends only on additivity of the Euler
46	% characteristic on a short exact sequence of coherent sheaves (a
47	% standard preadditive-category fact), the genus base case
48	% \(\chi(\mathcal O_C) = 1 - g\), and the closed-point skyscraper
49	% identity \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\).
50	
51	% NOTE (Genus-\(0\) carve-out vs. general \(g\) — iter-173 sub-phase
52	% choice). The chapter restricts the headline theorem
53	% REF to the genus-\(0\) specialisation
54	% \(\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1\) for \(\deg(D) \geq 0\). The general-\(g\)
55	% statement \(\ell(D) - \ell(K - D) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\) is downstream of
56	% \texttt{RR.2} (it requires the canonical divisor \(K\) and Serre
57	% duality / the \(K - D\) pairing) and is \emph{not} needed for the
58	% genus-\(0\) critical path: \texttt{RR.3} consumes only
59	% REF, and \texttt{RR.4} consumes
60	% \texttt{RR.3}. The intermediate \(\chi\)-identity
61	% REF is stated and
62	% sketched for general \(g\) (the inductive argument is genus-uniform);
63	% its full closure on a general-\(g\) curve waits for the project's
64	% subsequent Riemann--Roch chapter, but the proof body in
65	% \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RRFormula.lean} pins the
66	% \emph{statement} for general \(g\) and may close the body initially
67	% under a \(g = 0\) hypothesis.
68	
69	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, \(\bar k\) is an
70	algebraically closed field and \(C\) is a smooth proper geometrically
71	irreducible curve over \(\bar k\). Concretely, the Lean signatures
72	(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RRFormula.lean}) carry the same
73	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API:
74	\begin{quote}
75	  \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
76	   (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
77	   \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
78	   \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
79	\end{quote}
80	The genus \(g(C)\) is the one defined in REF of
81	\texttt{Genus.tex}, namely \(g(C) = \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\)
82	via the project's \(\Module\bar k\)-flavoured sheaf cohomology
83	(\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genus}).
84	
85	\section{The Euler characteristic of a coherent sheaf on a curve}
86	
87	\begin{definition}
88	
89	
90	\leanok
91	[Euler characteristic of a coherent sheaf on a curve]
92	  \label{def:eulerChar_curve}
93	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic}
94	  \uses{def:Scheme_HModule}
95	
96	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~295 (definition of \(\chi(\mathcal F)\)
97	  % inside the proof of Theorem~1.3) (read from
98	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 312).
99	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we have to show that for any \(D\),
100	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D)) = \deg D + 1 - g\),
101	  % where for any coherent sheaf \(\mathscr F\) on \(X\), \(\chi(\mathscr F)\)
102	  % is the Euler characteristic
103	  % \(\chi(\mathscr F) = \dim H^0(X, \mathscr F) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr F)\)."
104	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~295, the formula displayed
105	  inside the proof of Theorem~1.3.}
106	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\) and let \(\mathcal F\)
107	  be a coherent sheaf on \(C\). The \emph{Euler characteristic} of
108	  \(\mathcal F\) is the integer
109	  \[
110	    \chi(\mathcal F) \;:=\;
111	    \sum_{i \geq 0} (-1)^i \,\dim_{\bar k} H^i(C, \mathcal F)
112	    \;=\;
113	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal F)
114	    \,-\,
115	    \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal F).
116	  \]
117	  The second equality is a curve-specific simplification: because
118	  \(\dim C = 1\), Grothendieck vanishing (Hartshorne~III.2.7) gives
119	  \(H^i(C, \mathcal F) = 0\) for \(i \geq 2\), so the alternating sum has
120	  only two terms. Coherence of \(\mathcal F\) on the proper $\bar
121	  k\(-scheme \)C\( guarantees that both \)H^0\( and \)H^1$ are
122	  finite-dimensional \(\bar k\)-vector spaces (Serre's coherent-cohomology
123	  finiteness theorem, the same finiteness backing
124	  REF), so the two terms are honest natural numbers and
125	  the difference is a well-defined integer.
126	
127	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
128	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic} takes a coherent
129	  sheaf \(\mathcal F\) on \(C\) and returns an integer; the natural input
130	  shape is the project's \(\Module \bar k\)-valued sheaf-cohomology
131	  wrapper \(\HModule \bar k\) (REF) applied to a
132	  \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured carrier built from \(\mathcal F\), with
133	  \(\dim_{\bar k}\) extracted via \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\). The
134	  declaration is \texttt{noncomputable} (inherited from
135	  \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\) and from the \(\Ext\)-based cohomology
136	  construction).
137	\end{definition}
138	
139	\section{The \(\ell\)-invariant of a Weil divisor}
140	
141	\begin{definition}
142	
143	
144	\leanok
145	[\(\ell\)-invariant of a Weil divisor]
146	  \label{def:l_invariant}
147	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.l}
148	  \uses{def:Scheme_HModule, def:codim1_cycles, def:sheafOf}
149	
150	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~295 (definition of \(l(D)\) and of the
151	  % "dimension of \(|D|\)") (read from
152	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 312).
153	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We denote \(\dim_k H^0(X, \mathscr L(D))\) by \(l(D)\),
154	  % so that the \emph{dimension of \(|D|\)} is \(l(D) - 1\). The number
155	  % \(l(D)\) is finite by (II, 5.19) or (III, 5.2)."
156	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~295 (definition of \(l(D)\)).}
157	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\) and let
158	  \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) be a Weil divisor (REF). The
159	  \emph{\(\ell\)-invariant of \(D\)} is the integer
160	  \[
161	    \ell(D) \;:=\; \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))
162	    \;\in\; \mathbb N,
163	  \]
164	  the \(\bar k\)-dimension of the space of global sections of the
165	  invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) associated to \(D\). Finiteness of
166	  \(\ell(D)\) is a consequence of the coherent-cohomology finiteness
167	  theorem on a proper \(\bar k\)-scheme (Hartshorne~II.5.19 or
168	  III.5.2). The classical \emph{complete linear system \(|D|\)} has
169	  \(\bar k\)-dimension \(\ell(D) - 1\) as a projective space, but the
170	  chapter never uses the projective-space interpretation; \(\ell(D)\) is
171	  the only quantity it needs.
172	
173	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
174	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.l} accepts a divisor
175	  \(D : C.\mathtt{WeilDivisor}\) (REF) and returns
176	  \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\;\bar k\;(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D)))\),
177	  computed through the same \(\Module \bar k\)-valued sheaf-cohomology
178	  pipeline as REF. The associated invertible
179	  sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) is the line bundle of \texttt{RR.3} (sibling
180	  chapter, to be added); the present chapter's prose and Lean
181	  signature reference \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) by name and consume only its
182	  \(H^0\) functor. The declaration is \texttt{noncomputable}.
183	\end{definition}
184	
185	\section{The \(\chi\) identity: \(\chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\)}
186	
187	\subsection*{Substrate lemmas for the proof of the \(\chi\)-identity}
188	
189	The proof of REF below
190	decomposes into three substantive substrate lemmas, factored out of the
191	Hartshorne~IV.1.3 inductive argument. The first
192	(REF, \emph{additivity}) is the
193	classical statement that \(\chi\) descends to the Grothendieck group
194	of \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\); the second
195	(REF, \emph{iso-invariance}) is the
196	elementary fact that \(\chi\) is well defined on isomorphism classes
197	of sheaves; the third
198	(REF, \emph{skyscraper}) computes \(\chi\)
199	on a closed-point skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\). Pinning each as its own
200	Lean declaration lets the prover attack the three substrate gaps
201	independently of the parent inductive argument.
202	
203	\begin{lemma}\leanok
204	[Additivity of the Euler characteristic on a short exact sequence]
205	  \label{lem:euler_char_shortExact_add}
206	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_shortExact_add}
207	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve, def:Scheme_HModule}
208	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~296 (inside the proof of Theorem~1.3,
209	  % the citation ``III, Ex.~5.1'' for the additivity of \(\chi\) on a
210	  % short exact sequence) (read from
211	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 313).
212	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now the Euler characteristic is additive on short
213	  % exact sequences (III, Ex.~5.1), and \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\), so we have
214	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D + P)) = \chi(\mathscr L(D)) + 1\)."
215	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1 p.~296 (citation of III, Ex.~5.1
216	  inside the proof of Theorem~1.3).}
217	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
218	  \(\bar k\), and let
219	  \[
220	    0 \to \mathcal F_1 \to \mathcal F_2 \to \mathcal F_3 \to 0
221	  \]
222	  be a short exact sequence in the category of \(\Module \bar k\)-valued
223	  sheaves on \(C\). Then
224	  \[
225	    \chi(\mathcal F_2) \;=\; \chi(\mathcal F_1) + \chi(\mathcal F_3).
226	  \]
227	\end{lemma}
228	
229	\begin{proof}
230	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve}
231	  Apply the project-side \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured covariant Ext-LES
232	  (Mathlib's \texttt{Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence}) to the constant-sheaf
233	  test object \(L = (\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;J\;\_)(\Module.\mathtt{of}\;
234	  \bar k\;\bar k)\) and the short exact sequence
235	  \(0 \to \mathcal F_1 \to \mathcal F_2 \to \mathcal F_3 \to 0\). The
236	  resulting nine-term sequence
237	  \[
238	    H^0(\mathcal F_1) \to H^0(\mathcal F_2) \to H^0(\mathcal F_3)
239	    \to H^1(\mathcal F_1) \to H^1(\mathcal F_2) \to H^1(\mathcal F_3)
240	    \to H^2(\mathcal F_1) \to H^2(\mathcal F_2) \to H^2(\mathcal F_3)
241	  \]
242	  is exact in the category of \(\bar k\)-vector spaces. Grothendieck
243	  vanishing \(H^i(C, \mathcal F) = 0\) for \(i \geq 2\) on the
244	  one-dimensional scheme \(C\) (Hartshorne~III.2.7) truncates the
245	  sequence to a six-term exact sequence
246	  \[
247	    0 \to K_0 \to H^0(\mathcal F_1) \to H^0(\mathcal F_2) \to
248	    H^0(\mathcal F_3) \to H^1(\mathcal F_1) \to H^1(\mathcal F_2)
249	    \to H^1(\mathcal F_3) \to 0
250	  \]
251	  (with \(K_0 = 0\) the kernel of the first map, kept here only as a
252	  bookkeeping placeholder). Rank-counting on a six-term exact sequence
253	  of finite-dimensional \(\bar k\)-vector spaces — iterated applications
254	  of \(\mathtt{Submodule.finrank\_quotient\_add\_finrank}\) at each
255	  intermediate kernel/image quotient — yields the alternating identity
256	  \[
257	    \dim H^0(\mathcal F_1) - \dim H^0(\mathcal F_2) + \dim H^0(\mathcal F_3)
258	    - \dim H^1(\mathcal F_1) + \dim H^1(\mathcal F_2) - \dim H^1(\mathcal F_3)
259	    \;=\; 0,
260	  \]
261	  which rearranges, using the definition
262	  \(\chi(\mathcal F) = \dim H^0 - \dim H^1\)
263	  (REF), to
264	  \(\chi(\mathcal F_2) = \chi(\mathcal F_1) + \chi(\mathcal F_3)\).
265	
266	  % NOTE: gated on project-side LES carrier + finiteness machinery.
267	  % Mathlib `b80f227` ships `Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence` but not (a)
268	  % the `ModuleCat kbar`-flavoured LES carrier specialised to the
269	  % project's sheaf-cohomology pipeline `Scheme.HModule`, (b) Grothendieck
270	  % vanishing `H^i(C, F) = 0` for `i ≥ 2` at the `Ext`-cohomology level
271	  % consumed by `HModule`, nor (c) the six-term rank-counting bridge
272	  % composed of `Submodule.finrank_quotient_add_finrank` invocations.
273	  % The Lean helper `eulerCharacteristic_shortExact_add` therefore
274	  % carries a Tier-3 honest typed sorry; its closure lands once the
275	  % three substrate pieces are in place. Off the genus-0 critical path
276	  % (the parent theorem `thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus`
277	  % is the only consumer in `RR.2`).
278	\end{proof}
279	
280	\begin{lemma}\leanok
281	[Iso-invariance of the Euler characteristic]
282	  \label{lem:euler_char_iso}
283	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_iso}
284	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve}
285	  \textit{Project-bespoke decomposition} (Archon-original packaging of
286	  the Mathlib \(\Ext\)-postcomposition API; no external source named).
287	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
288	  \(\bar k\) and let \(\mathcal F, \mathcal G\) be \(\Module \bar k\)-valued
289	  sheaves on \(C\) with an isomorphism \(\mathcal F \cong \mathcal G\).
290	  Then
291	  \[
292	    \chi(\mathcal F) \;=\; \chi(\mathcal G).
293	  \]
294	\end{lemma}
295	
296	\begin{proof}
297	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve}
298	  \leanok
299	  An isomorphism \(e \colon \mathcal F \xrightarrow{\;\cong\;} \mathcal G\)
300	  induces, in each cohomological degree \(n \in \mathbb N\), a
301	  \(\bar k\)-linear equivalence
302	  \[
303	    H^n(C, \mathcal F) \;\xrightarrow{\;\cong\;}\; H^n(C, \mathcal G)
304	  \]
305	  by post-composition with the Ext-degree-zero classes
306	  \(\mathtt{Ext.mk}_0(e.\mathtt{hom})\) and
307	  \(\mathtt{Ext.mk}_0(e.\mathtt{inv})\). The mutual-inverse identities
308	  for this pair of post-compositions reduce — via the associativity
309	  lemma \(\mathtt{Ext.comp\_assoc\_of\_third\_deg\_zero}\), the
310	  composition lemma \(\mathtt{Ext.mk}_0\mathtt{\_comp\_mk}_0\), and the
311	  iso identities \(e.\mathtt{hom} \circ e.\mathtt{inv} = \mathrm{id}\),
312	  \(e.\mathtt{inv} \circ e.\mathtt{hom} = \mathrm{id}\) — to the identity
313	  map on \(\Ext\) elements. Specialising to \(n = 0\) and \(n = 1\) and
314	  applying \(\mathtt{LinearEquiv.finrank\_eq}\) at each of those two
315	  degrees gives
316	  \(\dim H^n(C, \mathcal F) = \dim H^n(C, \mathcal G)\) for
317	  \(n \in \{0, 1\}\). Unfolding the definition
318	  \(\chi(\mathcal F) = \dim H^0 - \dim H^1\)
319	  (REF) closes the goal.
320	\end{proof}

369	\begin{lemma}\leanok
370	[Euler characteristic of a closed-point skyscraper sheaf]
371	  \label{lem:euler_char_skyscraperSheaf}
372	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_skyscraperSheaf}
373	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve,
374	        lem:H0_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_one,
375	        lem:H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero}
376	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~296 (the \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\) input
377	  % cited inline inside the proof of Theorem~1.3) (read from
378	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 313).
379	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now the Euler characteristic is additive on short
380	  % exact sequences (III, Ex.~5.1), and \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\), so we have
381	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D + P)) = \chi(\mathscr L(D)) + 1\)."
382	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1 p.~296 (the ``\(\chi(k(P)) = 1\)''
383	  input inside the proof of Theorem~1.3).}
384	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
385	  \(\bar k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Write \(k(P)\) for
386	  the closed-point skyscraper sheaf
387	  \[
388	    k(P) \;:=\; \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;
389	    (\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k),
390	  \]
391	  the \(\Module \bar k\)-valued sheaf on \(C\) with stalk \(\bar k\) at
392	  \(P\) and zero stalks elsewhere. Then
393	  \[
394	    \chi(k(P)) \;=\; 1.
395	  \]
396	\end{lemma}
397	
398	\begin{proof}
399	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve,
400	        lem:H0_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_one,
401	        lem:H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero}
402	  \leanok
403	  By the definition of \(\chi\)
404	  (REF),
405	  \[
406	    \chi(k(P)) \;=\; \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, k(P))
407	    \;-\; \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, k(P)).
408	  \]
409	  The closure decomposes cleanly into two independent half-statements
410	  (\cref{lem:H0_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_one} and
411	  \cref{lem:H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero}), attacked separately
412	  by the prover.
413	
414	  \emph{H\(^0\) half} — \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, k(P)) = 1\).
415	  Compose the project's three-step \(\bar k\)-linear-equivalence chain
416	  \[
417	    H^0(C, k(P))
418	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\cong\;}\;
419	    \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{Sh}}\!\bigl(
420	      (\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;J\;\_)(\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k),\,
421	      k(P)
422	    \bigr)
423	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\cong\;}\;
424	    \Gamma(C, k(P))
425	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\cong\;}\;
426	    (k(P))(C),
427	  \]
428	  realised by the project lemmas
429	  \texttt{Scheme.HModule\_zero\_linearEquiv},
430	  \texttt{Scheme.constantSheafGammaHom\_linearEquiv}, and
431	  \texttt{Scheme.SheafGammaObj\_linearEquiv\_top} (the last step
432	  identifies the abstract \(\Gamma\) functor with the underlying
433	  presheaf evaluation at the top open \(\top\), i.e.\ at the whole
434	  underlying space of \(C\)). Applying
435	  \(\mathtt{LinearEquiv.finrank\_eq}\) along this chain reduces the
436	  goal to
437	  \(\dim_{\bar k} (k(P))(C) = 1\). The closed point
438	  \(P\) lies in the top open of \(C\), so the skyscraper presheaf
439	  evaluates at the top open to its stalk
440	  \(\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k\) by definition, and
441	  \(\dim_{\bar k} \bar k = 1\) closes the half. This half is
442	  axiom-clean closure projection (\(\sim 30\text{--}60\) LOC) against
443	  the existing project chain; it is the natural target for the
444	  iteration that follows the present blueprint expansion.
445	
446	  \emph{H\(^1\) half} — \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\).
447	  Mathematically, the closed-point skyscraper sheaf is flasque (any
448	  pushforward of a sheaf from a one-point space is flasque;
449	  Hartshorne~III.2.5(c)), and flasque sheaves have vanishing higher
450	  cohomology in degrees \(\geq 1\) on any topological space
451	  (Hartshorne~III.2.5). Hence \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\) as a
452	  \(\bar k\)-vector space.
453	
454	  % NOTE: gated on Mathlib flasque cohomology.
455	  % Mathlib `b80f227` does NOT ship an `H^n(X, F) = 0 for flasque F`
456	  % lemma at the `ModuleCat`-flavoured `Abelian.Ext`-cohomology level
457	  % used by the project's `Scheme.HModule kbar` pipeline. The closure
458	  % depends on either (a) an upstream Mathlib
459	  % `Sheaf.flasque_vanishing_cohomology` contribution at the
460	  % `Ext`-cohomology level for sheaves with values in an abelian
461	  % category with enough injectives, or (b) a project-side flasque-
462	  % cohomology bridge (~150-300 LOC, off the genus-0 critical path).
463	  % Until one of the two lands, the H¹ half is a typed-sorry blocker
464	  % independent of the H⁰ half — i.e. the prover can attack the H⁰
465	  % half against the existing project chain without waiting for the
466	  % H¹ machinery, and the present skyscraper lemma waits for the H¹
467	  % gap to clear.
468	
469	  Combining the two halves through the definition of \(\chi\),
470	  \(\chi(k(P)) = 1 - 0 = 1\), closes the lemma.
471	\end{proof}

473	\begin{lemma}[Global sections of the structure sheaf are one-dimensional]
474	  \label{lem:finrank_H0_toModuleKSheaf_eq_one}
475	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.finrank_H0_toModuleKSheaf_eq_one}
476	  \uses{def:Scheme_HModule, def:Scheme_toModuleKSheaf}
477	  \textit{Specialisation of Hartshorne~I.3.4 (``\(H^0(X, \mathcal O_X) = k\)
478	  for any projective variety over an algebraically closed field'') to the
479	  project's \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured cohomology pipeline.}
480	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
481	  \(\bar k\). The zeroth cohomology of the structure-sheaf carrier
482	  \(\toModuleKSheaf C\) is one-dimensional over \(\bar k\):
483	  \[
484	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
485	    \;=\; \dim_{\bar k}\, \HModule\,\bar k\,(\toModuleKSheaf C)\,0
486	    \;=\; 1.
487	  \]
488	  The proof identifies \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)\) with the global-sections
489	  ring \(\Gamma(C, \mathcal O_C) = \mathcal O_C(\top)\) through the
490	  project's \(\bar k\)-linear-equivalence chain
491	  (\cref{def:Scheme_HModule}). On the proper \(\bar k\)-scheme \(C\) this
492	  ring is a finite-dimensional \(\bar k\)-algebra; integrality of \(C\)
493	  makes it a domain; and a finite \(\bar k\)-algebra that is an integral
494	  domain over the algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) is \(\bar k\)
495	  itself, so its \(\bar k\)-dimension is \(1\).
496	\end{lemma}
497	
498	\begin{proof}
499	  Proved directly in Lean.
500	\end{proof}

502	\begin{lemma}[\(\chi\)-additivity across a short exact sequence with skyscraper cokernel]
503	  \label{lem:euler_char_of_shortExact_skyscraper}
504	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_of_shortExact_skyscraper}
505	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve, lem:euler_char_shortExact_add,
506	        lem:euler_char_iso, lem:euler_char_skyscraperSheaf}
507	  \textit{Project-bespoke packaging of the Hartshorne~IV.1.3 inductive
508	  step, combining the three substrate lemmas above.}
509	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
510	  \(\bar k\), let
511	  \[
512	    0 \to \mathcal F_1 \to \mathcal F_2 \to \mathcal F_3 \to 0
513	  \]
514	  be a short exact sequence of \(\Module \bar k\)-valued sheaves on
515	  \(C\), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point such that the cokernel
516	  term \(\mathcal F_3\) is isomorphic to the skyscraper sheaf
517	  \(k(P) = \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;(\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k)\).
518	  Then
519	  \[
520	    \chi(\mathcal F_2) \;=\; \chi(\mathcal F_1) + 1.
521	  \]
522	\end{lemma}
523	
524	\begin{proof}
525	  \uses{lem:euler_char_shortExact_add, lem:euler_char_iso,
526	        lem:euler_char_skyscraperSheaf}
527	  Additivity of the Euler characteristic across the short exact sequence
528	  (\cref{lem:euler_char_shortExact_add}) gives
529	  \(\chi(\mathcal F_2) = \chi(\mathcal F_1) + \chi(\mathcal F_3)\).
530	  Iso-invariance of \(\chi\) (\cref{lem:euler_char_iso}) applied to the
531	  isomorphism \(\mathcal F_3 \cong k(P)\) rewrites the last term as
532	  \(\chi(k(P))\), and the skyscraper computation
533	  (\cref{lem:euler_char_skyscraperSheaf}) evaluates \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\).
534	  Combining the three identities yields
535	  \(\chi(\mathcal F_2) = \chi(\mathcal F_1) + 1\).
536	\end{proof}

538	\subsection*{Inductive scaffolding via the divisor line bundle}
539	
540	The base case and the single-point inductive step of the
541	\(\chi\)-identity \cref{thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus} are
542	pinned as three named Lean lemmas. Each references the line bundle
543	\(\mathcal O_C(D)\) of \texttt{RR.3} (\cref{def:sheafOf}) and so carries a
544	typed-sorry transitivity inherited from that sibling chapter; the
545	arithmetic scaffolding around \(\chi\) is, however, honest.
546	
547	\begin{lemma}[Base case of the \(\chi\)-identity]
548	  \label{lem:euler_char_sheafOf_zero}
549	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_sheafOf_zero}
550	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve, lem:finrank_H0_toModuleKSheaf_eq_one,
551	        lem:sheafOf_zero, def:genus}
552	  \textit{Base case of Hartshorne~IV.1.3 (the \(D = 0\) step).}
553	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
554	  \(\bar k\). The Euler characteristic of the line bundle
555	  \(\mathcal O_C(0)\) of the zero divisor is
556	  \[
557	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(0)) \;=\; 1 - g(C).
558	  \]
559	\end{lemma}
560	
561	\begin{proof}
562	  \uses{lem:finrank_H0_toModuleKSheaf_eq_one, lem:sheafOf_zero, def:genus}
563	  The line bundle of the zero divisor is the structure-sheaf carrier,
564	  \(\mathcal O_C(0) = \toModuleKSheaf C\) (\cref{lem:sheafOf_zero}).
565	  Unfolding the two-term Euler characteristic
566	  \(\chi = \dim_{\bar k} H^0 - \dim_{\bar k} H^1\)
567	  (\cref{def:eulerChar_curve}) reduces the claim to
568	  \[
569	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
570	    - \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) \;=\; 1 - g(C).
571	  \]
572	  The first term equals \(1\)
573	  (\cref{lem:finrank_H0_toModuleKSheaf_eq_one}), and the second term is by
574	  definition the genus, \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = g(C)\)
575	  (\cref{def:genus}). Hence \(\chi(\mathcal O_C(0)) = 1 - g(C)\).
576	\end{proof}
577	
578	\begin{lemma}[Unit inductive step of the \(\chi\)-identity]
579	  \label{lem:euler_char_sheafOf_succ}
580	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_sheafOf_succ}
581	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve, lem:euler_char_of_shortExact_skyscraper,
582	        lem:sheafOf_ses_single_add, def:sheafOf}
583	  \textit{Single-point inductive step of Hartshorne~IV.1.3 (the
584	  ``\(D \leftrightsquigarrow D + P\)'' step, additive direction).}
585	  Let \(C\) be as above, let \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\), and let \(Y \in C\)
586	  be a prime (closed-point) divisor. Adding the single point \(Y\) raises
587	  the Euler characteristic by one:
588	  \[
589	    \chi(\mathcal O_C([Y] + D)) \;=\; \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) + 1.
590	  \]
591	\end{lemma}
592	
593	\begin{proof}
594	  \uses{lem:euler_char_of_shortExact_skyscraper, lem:sheafOf_ses_single_add}
595	  The closed-point short exact sequence of \texttt{RR.3}
596	  (\cref{lem:sheafOf_ses_single_add}) provides a short exact sequence
597	  \[
598	    0 \to \mathcal O_C(D) \to \mathcal O_C([Y] + D) \to k(Y) \to 0
599	  \]
600	  of \(\Module \bar k\)-valued sheaves whose cokernel is the closed-point
601	  skyscraper \(k(Y)\). Applying the packaged \(\chi\)-additivity with
602	  skyscraper cokernel (\cref{lem:euler_char_of_shortExact_skyscraper}) to
603	  this sequence yields
604	  \(\chi(\mathcal O_C([Y] + D)) = \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) + 1\).
605	\end{proof}
606	
607	\begin{lemma}[General single-point inductive step of the \(\chi\)-identity]
608	  \label{lem:euler_char_sheafOf_single_add}
609	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_sheafOf_single_add}
610	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve, lem:euler_char_sheafOf_succ, def:sheafOf}
611	  \textit{Iterated single-point step of Hartshorne~IV.1.3, both signs of
612	  the multiplicity.}
613	  Let \(C\) be as above, let \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\), let \(Y \in C\) be
614	  a prime divisor, and let \(n \in \mathbb Z\). Then
615	  \[
616	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(n[Y] + D)) \;=\; \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) + n.
617	  \]
618	\end{lemma}
619	
620	\begin{proof}
621	  \uses{lem:euler_char_sheafOf_succ}
622	  Induction on \(n \in \mathbb Z\). For \(n = 0\) the divisor \(n[Y]\)
623	  vanishes and the identity is trivial. For the positive direction,
624	  \((n+1)[Y] + D = [Y] + (n[Y] + D)\), so the unit step
625	  (\cref{lem:euler_char_sheafOf_succ}) and the inductive hypothesis give
626	  \[
627	    \chi(\mathcal O_C((n+1)[Y] + D))
628	    \;=\; \chi(\mathcal O_C(n[Y] + D)) + 1
629	    \;=\; \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) + (n+1).
630	  \]
631	  The negative direction runs the same unit step backwards: applying
632	  \cref{lem:euler_char_sheafOf_succ} to the divisor \((n-1)[Y] + D\) and
633	  rearranging gives
634	  \(\chi(\mathcal O_C((n-1)[Y] + D)) = \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) + (n-1)\),
635	  which is the inductive hypothesis transported one step in the negative
636	  direction.
637	\end{proof}
