639	\subsection*{The main \(\chi\)-identity}
640	
641	\begin{theorem}
642	
643	
644	\leanok
645	[Euler-characteristic identity for \(\mathcal O_C(D)\)]
646	  \label{thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus}
647	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_eq_degree_plus_one_minus_genus}
648	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve, def:l_invariant,
649	        lem:euler_char_shortExact_add, lem:euler_char_iso,
650	        lem:euler_char_skyscraperSheaf, def:genus,
651	        def:divisor_degree, def:divisor_closed_point, def:codim1_cycles}
652	
653	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, Theorem~1.3 (the reduction inside the
654	  % proof: ``Thus we have to show that for any \(D\), $\chi(\mathscr L(D))
655	  % = \deg D + 1 - g$'') (read from
656	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 312).
657	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Theorem 1.3 (Riemann--Roch). \emph{Let \(D\) be a
658	  % divisor on a curve \(X\) of genus \(g\). Then}
659	  % \(l(D) - l(K - D) = \deg D + 1 - g\)."
660	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we have to show that for any \(D\),
661	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D)) = \deg D + 1 - g\),
662	  % where for any coherent sheaf \(\mathscr F\) on \(X\), \(\chi(\mathscr F)\)
663	  % is the Euler characteristic
664	  % \(\chi(\mathscr F) = \dim H^0(X, \mathscr F) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr F)\)."
665	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Theorem~1.3, p.~295.}
666	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
667	  \(\bar k\) of genus \(g = g(C)\), and let \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\). Then
668	  \[
669	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 - g.
670	  \]
671	\end{theorem}
672	
673	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "First we consider the case \(D = 0\). Then our
674	% formula says
675	% \(\dim H^0(X, \mathscr O_X) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr O_X) = 1 - g\),
676	% because \(H^0(X, \mathscr O_X) = k\) for any projective variety
677	% (I, 3.4), and \(\dim H^1(X, \mathscr O_X) = g\) by (1.1).
678	% Next, let \(D\) be any divisor, and let \(P\) be any point. We will show
679	% that the formula is true for \(D\) if and only if it is true for
680	% \(D + P\). Since any divisor can be reached from \(0\) in a finite number
681	% of steps by adding or subtracting a point each time, this will show
682	% the result holds for all \(D\).
683	% We consider \(P\) as a closed subscheme of \(X\). Its structure sheaf is
684	% the skyscraper sheaf \(k\) sitting at the point \(P\), which we denote by
685	% \(k(P)\), and its ideal sheaf is \(\mathscr L(-P)\) by (II, 6.18).
686	% Therefore we have an exact sequence
687	% \(0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to k(P) \to 0\).
688	% Tensoring with \(\mathscr L(D + P)\) we get
689	% \(0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0\),
690	% (since \(\mathscr L(P)\) is locally free of rank 1, tensoring by it
691	% does not affect the sheaf \(k(P)\)). Now the Euler characteristic is
692	% additive on short exact sequences (III, Ex. 5.1), and $\chi(k(P))
693	% = 1$, so we have
694	% \(\chi(\mathscr L(D + P)) = \chi(\mathscr L(D)) + 1\).
695	% On the other hand, \(\deg D + P = \deg D + 1\), so our formula is true
696	% for \(D\) if and only if it is true for \(D + P\), as required."
697	\begin{proof}
698	  \leanok
699	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve, def:l_invariant,
700	    lem:euler_char_shortExact_add, lem:euler_char_iso,
701	    lem:euler_char_skyscraperSheaf}
702	
703	  The proof is by induction on the structure of \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\) as the free
704	  abelian group on closed points (REF,
705	  REF). Both sides of the asserted
706	  equation are additive in \(D\) (the right-hand side by linearity of
707	  \(\deg\), REF; the left-hand side by the
708	  inductive step proved below), so it suffices to verify the formula
709	  for \(D = 0\) and to show that it transports across the elementary
710	  modification \(D \mapsto D + [P]\) at any closed point \(P \in C\).
711	
712	  \emph{Base case \(D = 0\).} The line bundle \(\mathcal O_C(0)\) is the
713	  structure sheaf \(\mathcal O_C\), and the formula
714	  \(\chi(\mathcal O_C) = 1 - g\) reads
715	  \[
716	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
717	    - \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)
718	    \;=\; 1 - g.
719	  \]
720	  The second term is the definition of the genus,
721	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = g\) (REF). The
722	  first term is \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) = 1\): \(C\) is a
723	  proper geometrically irreducible curve over the algebraically
724	  closed field \(\bar k\), so its connected components agree with its
725	  geometrically connected components, and $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
726	  \cong \bar k\( as \)\bar k$-vector spaces (the Hartshorne~I.3.4
727	  statement that ``\(H^0(X, \mathcal O_X) = k\) for any projective
728	  variety'' over an algebraically closed field; the Mathlib home is
729	  the project's \(H^0\)-bridge in
730	  \texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK.tex}). Substituting these
731	  two values gives \(1 - g = 1 - g\), as required.
732	
733	  \emph{Inductive step (Hartshorne's ``\(D \leftrightsquigarrow D + P\)''
734	  step).} Let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. We follow Hartshorne's
735	  argument: view \(P\) as a closed subscheme of \(C\); its structure
736	  sheaf is the skyscraper sheaf \(k(P) \cong \bar k\) supported at \(P\),
737	  and its sheaf of ideals is \(\mathcal O_C(-[P]) \subset \mathcal O_C\)
738	  (the locally principal ideal sheaf cutting out \(P\) in \(C\)). Hence
739	  the short exact sequence
740	  \[
741	    0 \to \mathcal O_C(-[P]) \to \mathcal O_C \to k(P) \to 0
742	  \]
743	  in \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\) (Hartshorne~II.6.18). Tensoring with the
744	  invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])\) (a locally free
745	  \(\mathcal O_C\)-module of rank \(1\), so tensoring preserves exactness
746	  and leaves the skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\) unchanged) yields
747	  \[
748	    0 \to \mathcal O_C(D)
749	    \to \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
750	    \to k(P) \to 0.
751	  \]
752	  (Strictly: tensoring \(\mathcal O_C(-[P]) \subset \mathcal O_C\) with
753	  \(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])\) produces a sheaf isomorphic — but not
754	  syntactically equal — to \(\mathcal O_C(D)\), and the skyscraper
755	  factor \(k(P)\) is preserved up to isomorphism because
756	  \(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])\) is locally free of rank \(1\). We transport
757	  along this isomorphism via iso-invariance of \(\chi\),
758	  REF.)
759	  Additivity of \(\chi\) on the short exact sequence
760	  (REF, the project-side
761	  factoring of the ``Now the Euler characteristic is additive on short
762	  exact sequences (III, Ex.~5.1)'' input of Hartshorne~IV.1.3) gives
763	  \[
764	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D + [P]))
765	    \;=\;
766	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) + \chi(k(P)).
767	  \]
768	  The skyscraper-sheaf input
769	  \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\) is the standalone substrate lemma
770	  REF (factored from the
771	  same Hartshorne~IV.1.3 line); its proof splits into the
772	  H\(^0\) identification \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, k(P)) = 1\) and the
773	  H\(^1\) vanishing \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\), pursued
774	  independently by the prover (see the lemma's two-paragraph proof
775	  body). Therefore
776	  \[
777	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])) \;=\; \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) + 1.
778	  \]
779	  On the other hand, \(\deg([P]) = 1\) (every closed point contributes
780	  \(1\) to the bare-sum degree of REF), so by
781	  REF
782	  \[
783	    \deg(D + [P]) + 1 - g \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 - g + 1.
784	  \]
785	  Comparing the two displayed identities, the formula
786	  \(\chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\) holds for \(D\) if and only
787	  if it holds for \(D + [P]\). Since the closed-point divisors \([P]\)
788	  generate \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\) as a free abelian group, the formula propagates
789	  from \(D = 0\) to every \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) by induction on the (signed)
790	  number of generators in the expression of \(D\).
791	
792	  \emph{Lean reference note on the three substrate lemmas.} The
793	  inductive step of this proof consumes three substantive substrate
794	  lemmas, each pinned to its own Lean declaration in
795	  \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RRFormula.lean}: the additivity
796	  REF (\(\chi\) on a short
797	  exact sequence, gated on the project-side
798	  \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured Ext-LES carrier + Grothendieck
799	  vanishing + six-term rank-counting), the iso-invariance
800	  REF (\(\chi\) on
801	  isomorphism classes, axiom-clean against Mathlib's
802	  \(\Ext\)-postcomposition API; closed iter-186), and the skyscraper
803	  identity REF
804	  (\(\chi(k(P)) = 1\), splitting into an H\(^0\) half closed against
805	  the project's existing \(H^0\)-bridge and an H\(^1\) half gated on
806	  Mathlib flasque-cohomology — see the lemma's annotated proof body).
807	  Mathlib \texttt{b80f227} provides the Euler-characteristic API
808	  \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.EulerCharacteristic}
809	  (\texttt{HomologicalComplex.eulerChar},
810	  \texttt{GradedObject.eulerChar}) but does \emph{not} ship the
811	  specific additivity statement
812	  \texttt{CategoryTheory.ShortExact.eulerChar\_additive} at the
813	  \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured sheaf-cohomology level the project
814	  consumes; the additivity lemma is the project-side bridge.
815	\end{proof}
816	
817	\section{The Riemann--Roch formula in genus zero}
818	
819	\begin{theorem}
820	
821	
822	\leanok
823	[Riemann--Roch formula in genus zero]
824	  \label{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero}
825	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.l_eq_degree_plus_one_of_genus_zero}
826	  \uses{thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus, def:eulerChar_curve,
827	        def:l_invariant}
828	
829	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, Example~1.3.5 (genus-\(0\) specialisation
830	  % of Riemann--Roch; the curve-is-rational consequence) (read from
831	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 314).
832	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Example 1.3.5. Recall that a curve is \emph{rational}
833	  % if it is birational to \(\mathbf P^1\) (I, Ex. 6.1). Since curves in
834	  % this chapter are complete and nonsingular by definition, a curve
835	  % \(X\) is rational if and only if \(X \cong \mathbf P^1\) (I, 6.12). Now
836	  % using (1.3) we can show that \(X\) is rational if and only if \(g = 0\).
837	  % We already know that \(p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0\) (I, Ex. 7.2), so
838	  % conversely suppose given a curve \(X\) of genus 0. Let \(P, Q\) be two
839	  % distinct points of \(X\) and apply Riemann--Roch to the divisor
840	  % \(D = P - Q\). Since \(\deg K - D = -2\), using (1.3.3) above, we have
841	  % \(l(K - D) = 0\), and so we find \(l(D) = 1\). But \(D\) is a divisor of
842	  % degree 0, so by (1.2) we have \(D \sim 0\), in other words $P \sim
843	  % Q\(. But this implies \)X$ is rational (II, 6.10.1)."
844	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Example~1.3.5, p.~297.}
845	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
846	  \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) with
847	  \(\deg(D) \geq 0\). Then
848	  \[
849	    \ell(D) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1.
850	  \]
851	\end{theorem}
852	
853	\begin{proof}
854	  \leanok
855	  
856	  Specialising
857	  REF to the genus-\(0\)
858	  hypothesis \(g = 0\) yields
859	  \[
860	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1.
861	  \]
862	  Unfolding \(\chi\) via REF and the definition
863	  \(\ell(D) = \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))\)
864	  (REF), this rewrites as
865	  \[
866	    \ell(D) - \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(D))
867	    \;=\; \deg(D) + 1.
868	  \]
869	  Under the standing genus-\(0\) hypothesis and the assumption
870	  \(\deg(D) \geq 0\), the higher-cohomology term vanishes:
871	  \[
872	    H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(D)) \;=\; 0
873	    \qquad \text{for } \deg(D) \geq 0.
874	  \]
875	  Substituting yields \(\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1\), as required.
876	
877	  \emph{The \(H^1\)-vanishing input.} On a smooth proper geometrically
878	  irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), the vanishing
879	  \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(D)) = 0\) for \(\deg(D) \geq 0\) is the Hartshorne
880	  formulation Lemma~1.2 read covariantly: \(\ell(D) \neq 0\) forces
881	  \(\deg(D) \geq 0\); equivalently, for a curve where every
882	  degree-\(0\)-or-higher invertible sheaf is generated by global
883	  sections after a single point-twist, \(H^1\) of the twist must vanish
884	  by the long exact sequence of the skyscraper extension at any
885	  closed point. In the chain of sub-build chapters, this \(H^1\)
886	  vanishing is the natural content of \texttt{RR.3} (sibling chapter
887	  \texttt{RiemannRoch\_OcOfD.tex}, to be added), where the line
888	  bundle \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) and its cohomology are constructed; once
889	  \texttt{RR.3} is closed, the present theorem inherits the
890	  vanishing as an instance, and the proof closes verbatim as
891	  displayed. Pending \texttt{RR.3}, the Lean signature
892	  \texttt{l\_eq\_degree\_plus\_one\_of\_genus\_zero} threads the
893	  \(H^1\)-vanishing hypothesis explicitly as a named premise; the
894	  iteration that closes \texttt{RR.3} then drops the premise in a
895	  follow-up signature simplification.
896	
897	  \emph{Sub-build note for \texttt{RR.4}.} The downstream consumer of
898	  this theorem is the genus-\(0\) classification
899	  REF of
900	  \texttt{AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex}: with \(\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1\)
901	  and the degree-zero principal-divisor identity
902	  (REF), Hartshorne's Example~1.3.5 chain
903	  produces a degree-\(1\) morphism \(C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) via
904	  \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} applied to the two-dimensional
905	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))\), which is then upgraded to an
906	  isomorphism in \texttt{RR.4}.
907	\end{proof}
908	
909	\section{Out of scope}
910	
911	The following items are deliberately \emph{not} part of \texttt{RR.2}
912	and are sequenced into the sibling sub-build chapters:
913	
914	\begin{itemize}
915	  \item The general-\(g\) Riemann--Roch theorem
916	    \(\ell(D) - \ell(K - D) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\) (Hartshorne~IV.1.3 in
917	    its full form). This requires the canonical divisor \(K\), the
918	    canonical sheaf \(\Omega^1_{C / \bar k} = \omega_C\), and
919	    Serre duality $H^1(C, \mathcal L) \cong H^0(C, \omega_C \otimes
920	    \mathcal L^{-1})^*$. Mathlib has none of these for schemes, and
921	    the genus-\(0\) critical path does not require them; the general
922	    statement is queued for a follow-up chapter (post-RR-bridge).
923	  \item The construction of the invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\)
924	    from a Weil divisor \(D\), the linear-equivalence isomorphism
925	    \(\mathcal O_C(D) \cong \mathcal O_C(D')\) when \(D \sim D'\), and
926	    the computation of $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P])) \cong \bar k \cdot
927	    1 \oplus \bar k \cdot t$ (\emph{a non-constant rational function
928	    \(t\) exists}) live in \texttt{RR.3} (sibling chapter
929	    \texttt{RiemannRoch\_OcOfD.tex}, to be added). Inside the proof
930	    of REF the dependency on
931	    \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) is reduced to its \(H^0\) functor and the short
932	    exact sequence of the closed point.
933	  \item The vanishing \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(D)) = 0\) for
934	    \(\deg(D) \geq 0\) on a genus-\(0\) curve is consumed as a named
935	    premise by REF; its closure is
936	    part of \texttt{RR.3} (where the cohomology of
937	    \(\mathcal O_{\mathbb P^1}(d)\) is computed) and is recovered for
938	    a general smooth proper genus-\(0\) curve via the isomorphism
939	    \(C \cong \mathbb P^1\) of \texttt{RR.4} or directly via the
940	    base-change argument inside \texttt{RR.3}.
941	  \item The classification ``a smooth proper geometrically irreducible
942	    curve over \(\bar k\) with \(g = 0\) is isomorphic to
943	    \(\mathbb P^1\)'' (Hartshorne Example~IV.1.3.5 proper) is the
944	    headline of \texttt{RR.4} (sibling chapter
945	    \texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalIsoP1.tex}, to be added). The
946	    present chapter supplies one of its two inputs (the $\ell(D) =
947	    \deg(D) + 1\( identity); the other input is the degree-\)0$
948	    principal-divisor identity REF from
949	    \texttt{RR.1}.
950	  \item Higher-cohomology bookkeeping (\(\chi\) as an alternating sum
951	    over all \(i \geq 0\), the Grothendieck vanishing
952	    \(H^i(C, \mathcal F) = 0\) for \(i > \dim C = 1\)) is not separately
953	    formalised here; the chapter consumes only the two-term form
954	    \(\chi(\mathcal F) = \dim H^0 - \dim H^1\) which is the standing
955	    curve specialisation.
956	\end{itemize}

2	\label{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}

27	% \emph{sheafify} the resulting group-valued presheaf

35	\section{Setup and motivation}
36	\label{sec:relpic_setup}
37	
38	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
39	\(k\). As in \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}, write
40	\(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) for the projection of a relative curve over a
41	\(k\)-scheme \(T\) and
42	\(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) for the induced pullback of
43	isomorphism classes of line bundles (\cref{def:pullback_along_projection}).
44	
45	The set-valued relative Picard presheaf
46	\[
47	  \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
48	\]
49	of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} was set up forgetting the
50	abelian-group structure inherited from tensor product. This chapter restores
51	that structure and then sheafifies. The first half (\S~REF
52	and \S~REF) verifies that the quotient is canonically
53	an abelian group and the functoriality of \cref{thm:pullback_natural} respects
54	that group structure; the result is a group-valued presheaf
55	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Ab\) on the category of \(k\)-schemes. The
56	second half (\S~REF) takes the \'etale sheafification
57	\(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} := (\Pic^\sharp_{C/k})^\sim\) in the \'etale topology on
58	\((\Sch/k)^{op}\), and verifies that sheafification preserves the abelian-group
59	target. The \'etale-sheafified functor is the object represented by the Picard
60	scheme in \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}.
61	
62	\section{Group structure on the relative Picard quotient}
63	\label{sec:relpic_group}
64	
65	The Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) of any scheme is canonically an abelian group under
66	tensor product of line bundles (Stacks tag 01CR; the inverse of \([\mathcal{L}]\)
67	is $[\mathcal{L}^{-1}] = [\mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L},
68	\mathcal{O}_X)]$). The pullback map
69	\(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) of
70	\cref{def:pullback_along_projection} respects this structure: pullback of
71	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules sends \(\mathcal{O}_T\) to \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)
72	and is multiplicative on tensor products.
73	
74	\begin{lemma}\leanok
75	[Group structure descends to the relative Picard quotient]
76	  \label{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
77	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup}
78	  \uses{def:line_bundle_on_product, def:pullback_along_projection,
79	    thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
80	
81	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
82	  % Definition df:aPf + Definition df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard
83	  % functors are \emph{abelian-group}-valued, by construction; the relative
84	  % Picard functor is defined as a quotient \emph{group})
85	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
86	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
87	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
88	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
89	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
90	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)
91	  %  [\dots]
92	  %  \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
93	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
94	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)"
95	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
96	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the Picard functors are abelian-group-valued, and the
97	  relative one is a quotient of abelian groups; cf.\ Stacks tag 01CR for the
98	  abelian-group structure on \(\Pic\)).}
99	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve and let \(T\) be a
100	  \(k\)-scheme. The Picard group \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is an abelian group under
101	  tensor product of line bundles, and the pullback map
102	  \[
103	    \pi_T^* \;\colon\; \Pic(T) \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic(C \times_k T)
104	  \]
105	  of \cref{def:pullback_along_projection} is a group homomorphism. Its image
106	  \[
107	    H_T \;\;:=\;\; \pi_T^* \Pic(T)
108	  \]
109	  is therefore a subgroup of \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\), and the quotient set of
110	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
111	  \[
112	    \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, H_T
113	  \]
114	  inherits a canonical abelian-group structure under which the quotient map
115	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a surjective
116	  homomorphism with kernel exactly \(H_T\).
117	\end{lemma}
118

143	\begin{proof}
144	  \uses{def:line_bundle_on_product, def:pullback_along_projection,

146	    lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial, lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,
147	    lem:tensorobj_comm_iso, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
148	    lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup, lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible,
149	    lem:pullback_unit_iso, lem:pullback_tensor_iso_loctriv}
150	
151	
152	\leanok
153	  The construction proceeds in four steps over the locally-trivial carrier
154	  \[
155	    \mathrm{OnProduct}(\pi_C, \pi_T) \;=\;
156	      \bigl\{\, M : \text{a module on } C \times_k T \;\bigm|\;
157	      M \text{ is locally trivial} \,\bigr\}
158	  \]
159	  of \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}, on whose isomorphism classes the
160	  relative Picard quotient is taken. It is authored \emph{in parallel} against
161	  two inputs being built concurrently: the locally-trivial pullback--tensor
162	  comparison isomorphism \cref{lem:pullback_tensor_iso_loctriv} (consumed in
163	  Step~2 to make \(\pi_T^*\) additive) and the tensor inverse
164	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} (consumed in Step~1 for group
165	  closure); both are themselves deferred targets.
166	
167	  \emph{Step 1 (abelian group on the carrier).} On isomorphism classes of the
168	  carrier \(\mathrm{OnProduct}(\pi_C, \pi_T)\), addition is the descent of the
169	  scheme-level tensor product of line bundles,
170	  \[
171	    [L] + [L'] \;:=\; [\, L \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} L' \,],
172	  \]
173	  where \(L \otimes L'\) is the locally-trivial descent of the scheme-level
174	  tensor product (\cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}); the sum lands again in
175	  the carrier because the tensor product of two locally-trivial modules is
176	  locally trivial (\cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}). The zero
177	  element is the class \([\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}]\) of the structure sheaf,
178	  the monoidal unit, which is locally trivial. Associativity, commutativity,
179	  and left/right unitality of \(+\) descend from the scheme-level coherence
180	  isomorphisms --- the associator (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), the
181	  braiding (\cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}), and the unitors
182	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}) --- exactly as for the absolute Picard group
183	  on tensor-invertible isomorphism classes
184	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}). The additive inverse is
185	  \[
186	    -[L] \;:=\; [\, L^{\mathrm{inv}} \,],
187	  \]
188	  where \(L^{\mathrm{inv}}\) is the witness supplied by
189	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}: it returns a \emph{locally-trivial}
190	  module together with an isomorphism
191	  \(L \otimes L^{\mathrm{inv}} \cong \mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\), so the inverse
192	  stays inside the loc-triv carrier and the group is closed under negation.
193	
194	  \emph{Step 2 (the pullback homomorphism \(\pi_T^*\)).} Pullback along the
195	  projection \(\pi_T\) gives an additive map
196	  \[
197	    \pi_T^* \;\colon\; \Pic(T) \;\longrightarrow\; \mathrm{OnProduct}(\pi_C, \pi_T),
198	  \]
199	  whose underlying assignment of line bundles is
200	  \cref{def:pullback_along_projection}. It is a homomorphism of abelian groups:
201	  it sends the unit to the unit, since
202	  \(\pi_T^*\mathcal{O}_T \cong \mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) by the unconditional
203	  pullback--unit isomorphism (\cref{lem:pullback_unit_iso}); and it is additive,
204	  since pullback commutes with tensor product on locally-trivial modules,
205	  \[
206	    \pi_T^*(N \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_T} N') \;\cong\;
207	      \pi_T^* N \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \pi_T^* N',
208	  \]
209	  by the locally-trivial comparison isomorphism
210	  \cref{lem:pullback_tensor_iso_loctriv} specialised to the projection
211	  \(\pi_T\) on the \(\mathrm{OnProduct}\) carrier. This is the exact analogue,
212	  for the geometric pullback, of the additive monoid homomorphism on Picard
213	  groups induced by a ring map respecting tensor product, and is modelled on it
214	  field-for-field. Write \(H_T := \mathrm{im}(\pi_T^*)\) for its image, a
215	  subgroup of the carrier's isomorphism-class group.
216	
217	  \emph{Step 3 (setoid reconciliation).} The set-valued quotient of
218	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} is taken modulo the
219	  isomorphism-class equivalence that declares two line bundles related when
220	  their classes differ by an element of \(H_T\). This relation coincides with
221	  the left-coset relation of the subgroup \(H_T\) inside the abelian group of
222	  Steps~1--2: both encode
223	  \[
224	    L \sim L' \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
225	      [\, L^{\mathrm{inv}} \otimes L' \,] \in H_T .
226	  \]
227	  Establishing this equivalence of relations is a concrete isomorphism-class
228	  argument over the carrier; it consumes the tensor inverse (Step~1) and the
229	  group law, but not the computational content of the comparison isomorphism.
230	
231	  \emph{Step 4 (transport).} By Step~3 the quotient set underlying
232	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is in canonical bijection with the group quotient
233	  \(\mathrm{OnProduct}(\pi_C, \pi_T) / H_T\), which carries the canonical
234	  abelian-group structure of a quotient of an abelian group by a subgroup.
235	  Transporting this structure along the bijection of Step~3 endows
236	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) with its abelian-group structure, under which the
237	  canonical surjection
238	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a
239	  homomorphism with kernel exactly \(H_T\).
240	\end{proof}

242	\subsection{Helper constructions underlying the group law}
243	\label{subsec:relpic_group_helpers}
244	
245	The abelian-group instance of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} is assembled
246	from a handful of project-internal helper constructions on the locally-trivial
247	carrier \(\mathrm{OnProduct}(\pi_C, \pi_T)\): the tensor-product operation that
248	realises addition, the local triviality of the unit (the zero element), the
249	uniqueness of tensor inverses (which makes negation well defined), and the two
250	descended operations on isomorphism classes. Each is recorded here for the
251	one-to-one Lean correspondence; all are proved directly in Lean.
252	
253	\begin{definition}[Tensor product on relative line bundles]
254	  \label{def:rel_tensor_obj}
255	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.relTensorObj}
256	  \uses{def:line_bundle_on_product, lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
257	    lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
258	  For relative line bundles \(L, L'\) on the locally-trivial carrier
259	  \(\mathrm{OnProduct}(\pi_C, \pi_T)\) of \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product},
260	  their \emph{tensor product} is
261	  \[
262	    L \,\mathbin{\otimes}\, L' \;:=\;
263	      L \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}} L',
264	  \]
265	  the locally-trivial descent of the scheme-level tensor product
266	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}). The result again lies in the carrier
267	  because the tensor product of two locally-trivial modules is locally trivial
268	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}). This is the operation that
269	  realises addition \([L] + [L'] := [L \otimes L']\) on isomorphism classes.
270	\end{definition}
271	
272	\begin{lemma}[The structure-sheaf unit is locally trivial]
273	  \label{lem:rel_pic_sharp_unit_loctriv}
274	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.isLocallyTrivial_unit}
275	  \uses{def:line_bundle_on_product, lem:pullback_unit_iso}
276	  The structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}\) (the monoidal unit) is
277	  locally trivial, hence defines an element of the carrier
278	  \(\mathrm{OnProduct}(\pi_C, \pi_T)\). Its isomorphism class is the zero
279	  element of the group of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
280	\end{lemma}
281	
282	\begin{proof}
283	  \uses{lem:pullback_unit_iso}
284	  Proved directly in Lean. Local triviality is checked on an affine chart: for
285	  any point \(x\), choose an affine open \(W \ni x\); the restriction of the
286	  structure sheaf to \(W\) is its pullback along the inclusion
287	  \(W \hookrightarrow C \times_S T\), and the pullback of the structure sheaf is
288	  again the structure sheaf by the unconditional pullback--unit isomorphism
289	  (\cref{lem:pullback_unit_iso}). Composing these two isomorphisms trivialises
290	  \((\mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T})|_W \cong \mathcal{O}_W\).
291	\end{proof}
292	
293	\begin{lemma}[Uniqueness of the tensor inverse]
294	  \label{lem:rel_pic_sharp_inverse_unique}
295	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.pInverseUnique}
296	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
297	    lem:tensorobj_comm_iso, lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}
298	  Let \(M, N, N'\) be relative line bundles, and suppose there are
299	  isomorphisms
300	  \[
301	    M \otimes N \;\cong\; \mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}
302	    \qquad\text{and}\qquad
303	    M \otimes N' \;\cong\; \mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}.
304	  \]
305	  Then \(N \cong N'\). In other words, the tensor inverse of a relative line
306	  bundle is unique up to isomorphism.
307	\end{lemma}
308	
309	\begin{proof}
310	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
311	    lem:tensorobj_comm_iso, lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}
312	  Proved directly in Lean. One transports \(N\) across the chain of coherence
313	  isomorphisms
314	  \[
315	    N \;\cong\; N \otimes \mathcal{O}
316	      \;\cong\; N \otimes (M \otimes N')
317	      \;\cong\; (N \otimes M) \otimes N'
318	      \;\cong\; (M \otimes N) \otimes N'
319	      \;\cong\; \mathcal{O} \otimes N'
320	      \;\cong\; N',
321	  \]
322	  using the right unitor and left unitor (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}), the
323	  given trivialisations together with bifunctoriality of \(\otimes\)
324	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}), the associator
325	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), and the braiding
326	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). This is the relative-line-bundle analogue of
327	  the standard uniqueness of inverses in the Picard group.
328	\end{proof}
329	
330	\begin{definition}[Descended addition on the relative Picard quotient]
331	  \label{def:rel_add}
332	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.relAdd}
333	  \uses{def:rel_tensor_obj, thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
334	    lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
335	  The addition on the relative Picard quotient
336	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) = \Pic(C \times_S T)/\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) is the descent of
337	  the tensor product \cref{def:rel_tensor_obj} to isomorphism classes:
338	  \[
339	    [L] + [L'] \;:=\; [\,L \otimes L'\,].
340	  \]
341	\end{definition}
342	
343	\begin{proof}
344	  \uses{def:rel_tensor_obj, lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
345	  Proved directly in Lean. Well-definedness on the quotient
346	  (\cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}) is bifunctoriality of the
347	  tensor product: replacing \(L, L'\) by isomorphic representatives
348	  \(L \cong M\), \(L' \cong M'\) yields an isomorphism
349	  \(L \otimes L' \cong M \otimes M'\) (\cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}),
350	  so the class \([L \otimes L']\) is independent of the chosen representatives.
351	\end{proof}
352	
353	\begin{definition}[Descended negation on the relative Picard quotient]
354	  \label{def:rel_neg}
355	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.relNeg}
356	  \uses{def:rel_tensor_obj, lem:rel_pic_sharp_inverse_unique,
357	    lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}
358	  The negation on the relative Picard quotient is
359	  \[
360	    -[L] \;:=\; [\,L^{\mathrm{inv}}\,],
361	  \]
362	  where \(L^{\mathrm{inv}}\) is the locally-trivial tensor-inverse witness
363	  supplied by \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}, together with its
364	  trivialisation \(L \otimes L^{\mathrm{inv}} \cong \mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}\).
365	\end{definition}
366	
367	\begin{proof}
368	  \uses{lem:rel_pic_sharp_inverse_unique, lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}
369	  Proved directly in Lean. Well-definedness on the quotient uses uniqueness of
370	  the tensor inverse (\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_inverse_unique}): if
371	  \(L \cong M\), then the chosen inverse witnesses \(L^{\mathrm{inv}}\) and
372	  \(M^{\mathrm{inv}}\) of \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} both trivialise
373	  the same class up to the isomorphism \(L \cong M\), hence
374	  \(L^{\mathrm{inv}} \cong M^{\mathrm{inv}}\), so \([L^{\mathrm{inv}}]\) is
375	  independent of the representative.
376	\end{proof}

570	\section{\'Etale sheafification}
571	\label{sec:relpic_etale_sheaf}
572	
573	The presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) is generally not an \'etale (or even Zariski)
574	sheaf. The standard obstruction (Kleiman \S 2, L1292--L1302): for a \(k\)-scheme
575	\(T\) carrying a non-trivial line bundle whose pullback to \(C \times_k T\) becomes
576	non-trivial after an open cover, the Zariski separated-presheaf condition
577	fails. Therefore, to obtain a representable functor in the sense of
578	\cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}, one must replace
579	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) by its \emph{associated \'etale sheaf}. Kleiman \S 2 names
580	this sheaf \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\) and singles it out as the functor that Kleiman's
581	main existence theorem \S 4 (th:main) represents.
582

585	  \label{def:rel_pic_etale_sheafification}
586	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.etSheaf}

591	  % ``Picard_FGAPicRepresentability`` (which legitimately uses ``PicScheme``
592	  % for the REPRESENTING SCHEME, a different mathematical object). The
593	  % ``\lean{...}`` pin is updated accordingly. The §"Lean encoding" bullet 5
594	  % below is also corrected.

598	  % associated to the relative Picard functor

620	  associated-sheaf paragraph immediately following df:Pfs.}
621	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve. The
622	  \emph{\'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor} is the sheafification of
623	  the presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) (\cref{thm:rel_pic_sharp_presheaf}) in the
624	  global \'etale topology on \((\Sch/k)\):

630	  isomorphism) sheaf of abelian groups on the \'etale site of \(\Sch/k\)
631	  equipped with a presheaf morphism $\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \to
632	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ which is universal among presheaf morphisms from
633	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) to \'etale sheaves of abelian groups. In Kleiman's

636	  extension \(k'/k\), the natural map
637	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\) is a bijection (Kleiman
638	  Exercise ex:Alr, L1357--L1361).
639	\end{definition}

756	\begin{theorem}[Canonical sheafification unit with universal property (forward-looking)]
757	  \label{thm:rel_pic_etale_sheaf_unit_canonical}
758	  % \lean: TODO placeholder per DAG integrity rule 1. This is the forward-looking
759	  % universal property of the canonical étale sheafification unit; no standalone
760	  % Lean declaration realises it yet (RelPicFunctor.lean provides PicSharp.etSheaf
761	  % and etSheaf_group_structure, but not the universal-property statement), so a
762	  % placeholder name keeps the node rule-1 compliant.
763	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.TODO.rel_pic_etale_sheaf_unit_canonical}
764	  \uses{def:rel_pic_sharp, def:rel_pic_etale_sheafification}

766	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
767	  % Def.~df:Pfs + the associated-sheaf paragraph immediately following
768	  % (the \'etale sheaf is characterised as the sheaf associated to the
769	  % relative Picard functor, via the natural map from a presheaf to its
770	  % associated sheaf --- i.e.\ the sheafification unit with its universal
771	  % property)
772	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1328).
773	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
774	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
775	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
776	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)
777	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
778	  % topologies  by
779	  %     \(\)\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
780	  %     \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.\(\)
781	  %  \end{dfn}
782	  %  [\dots]
783	  %  if the latter functor is one of the three just displayed,
784	  %  then it is a sheaf in the indicated topology; in fact, it is the sheaf
785	  %  associated to any one of its predecessors, and the map between them is
786	  %  the natural map from a presheaf to its associated sheaf, as is easy to
787	  %  check.  In particular, the three displayed sheaves are the sheaves
788	  %  associated to the absolute Picard functor \(\Pic_X\), as well as to the
789	  %  relative Picard functor \(\Pic_{X/S}\)."
790	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
791	  Def.~df:Pfs together with the associated-sheaf paragraph
792	  immediately following (the natural map from a presheaf to its
793	  associated sheaf, taken inside the category of abelian groups, is
794	  universal among presheaf morphisms from \(\Pic_{X/S}\) into \'etale
795	  sheaves of abelian groups).}
796	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve. There
797	  exists a canonical morphism of presheaves of abelian groups
798	  \[
799	    \eta \;\colon\; \Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \;\longrightarrow\;
800	    \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}
801	  \]
802	  with the following universal property. For every \'etale sheaf
803	  \(\mathcal{F}\) of abelian groups on \((\Sch/k)\) and every morphism
804	  of presheaves of abelian groups
805	  \(\varphi : \Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \to \mathcal{F}\), there is a unique
806	  morphism of \'etale sheaves of abelian groups
807	  \(\tilde\varphi : \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \to \mathcal{F}\) such that
808	  \(\tilde\varphi \circ \eta = \varphi\). Equivalently, the pair
809	  \(\bigl(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}, \, \eta\bigr)\) is initial among
810	  pairs \((\mathcal{F}, \, \Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \to \mathcal{F})\)
811	  consisting of an \'etale sheaf of abelian groups on \((\Sch/k)\) and
812	  a morphism of presheaves of abelian groups from \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\)
813	  to \(\mathcal{F}\). In particular, the abelian-group structure on
814	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\) at each \(k\)-scheme \(T\) is uniquely
815	  determined (up to canonical isomorphism) by the abelian-group
816	  structure on \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) and the universal property of
817	  \(\eta\).
818	\end{theorem}
819	
820	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: As with df:Pfs itself, Kleiman \S 2 does not
821	% supply a separate proof body --- the universal property is built
822	% into ``the sheaf associated to'' as a synonym for sheafification,
823	% as the verbatim quote above already makes plain. The project-side
824	% argument is the standard universal property of sheafification in a
825	% Grothendieck topology, transported through the colimit-preservation
826	% of \(\AddCommGroup \to \mathbf{Set}\).
827	\begin{proof}
828	
829	  In any Grothendieck topology, every presheaf has a canonical
830	  associated sheaf, and the canonical morphism from a presheaf to its
831	  associated sheaf is universal among morphisms from the presheaf into
832	  sheaves (Stacks tag 00WI; Kleiman \S 2, paragraph following
833	  df:Pfs). The \'etale site of \((\Sch/k)\) is a Grothendieck
834	  topology, so this applies in particular to \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) and
835	  yields the canonical set-valued sheafification
836	  \((\Pic^\sharp_{C/k})^{\sim_\et}\) together with the universal arrow
837	  out of \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\). The forgetful functor
838	  \(\AddCommGroup \to \mathbf{Set}\) preserves and reflects the
839	  filtered colimits and finite limits used by the plus-construction,
840	  so performing the same plus-construction inside \(\AddCommGroup\)
841	  yields a sheaf of abelian groups whose underlying set-valued sheaf
842	  coincides with the set-valued sheafification; the abelian-group
843	  structure on the sheafification is transported back through this
844	  identification. The resulting morphism \(\eta\) is then by
845	  construction the universal arrow in the abelian-group-valued
846	  sheafification, which is the universal property in the form stated.
847	\end{proof}

849	\section{Lean encoding}
850	\label{sec:relpic_lean_encoding}
851	
852	The Lean target file is the new
853	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelPicFunctor.lean}, sibling to
854	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/LineBundlePullback.lean}
855	(\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}) and
856	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean}
857	(\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}). The blueprint declarations correspond to
858	Lean targets as follows.
859	\begin{itemize}
860	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup}
861	    (\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}) is the abelian-group instance on the
862	    quotient set built in
863	    \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.preimage\_subgroup}
864	    (\cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}). Mathlib's
865	    \texttt{QuotientAddGroup} machinery on a normal subgroup of an abelian
866	    group is the backbone; the project-side work is to certify that
867	    \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism (one extra
868	    \texttt{LinearMap.map\_mul}--style line on tensor product preservation).
869	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp}
870	    (\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}) is the group-valued presheaf
871	    \(T \mapsto \Pic(C \times_k T)/\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\). The Lean form is a functor
872	    \((\Sch/k)^{op} \to \AddCommGroup\) built from the data of

874	    \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
875	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.functorial}
876	    (\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_functorial}) packages the structure-map
877	    homomorphism property: the set map \(g^\sharp\) of

879	    this is a one-step strengthening of the existing set-valued naturality
880	    proof.
881	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.presheaf}
882	    (\cref{thm:rel_pic_sharp_presheaf}) wraps the previous three lemmas as a
883	    functor instance into \(\AddCommGroup\).
884	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.etSheaf}
885	    % NOTE (iter-176 review): renamed from ``AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme``
886	    % to avoid a collision with the representing-scheme declaration of the same
887	    % name in ``Picard_FGAPicRepresentability``.
888	    (\cref{def:rel_pic_etale_sheafification}) is the \'etale sheafification
889	    of \texttt{PicSharp}. The Lean form is
890	    \texttt{CategoryTheory.presheafToSheaf} applied to the big \'etale
891	    topology on \texttt{Scheme} with target category
892	    \texttt{AddCommGrpCat}.
893	    \textbf{Lean API note.} The big \'etale Grothendieck topology on the
894	    category of schemes is
895	    \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.etaleTopology}, of type
896	    \texttt{CategoryTheory.GrothendieckTopology AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme}
897	    (module \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Sites.Etale}; an
898	    \texttt{abbrev} for the topology generated by the big \'etale

900	    entry point with abelian-group target is
901	    \texttt{CategoryTheory.presheafToSheaf
902	    AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.etaleTopology AddCommGrpCat}, of type
903	    \texttt{Functor (Schemeᵒᵖ \(\Rightarrow\) AddCommGrpCat)
904	    (Sheaf etaleTopology AddCommGrpCat)} (module
905	    \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Sites.Sheafification}); it is
906	    available under a \texttt{CategoryTheory.HasWeakSheafify} instance
907	    for the pair \texttt{(etaleTopology, AddCommGrpCat)}. The slice
908	    \((\Sch/k)\) on the Lean side is captured by working over the
909	    global \texttt{Scheme} category and restricting along
910	    \texttt{Over (Spec k)} via Mathlib's
911	    \texttt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Over} / \texttt{GrothendieckTopology}
912	    pullback construction (the relative Picard functor's natural domain
913	    is the slice; Kleiman's notation \((\Sch/S)^{op}\) corresponds to
914	    \texttt{(Over (Spec k))ᵒᵖ}). If the \texttt{HasWeakSheafify}
915	    instance for the pair \texttt{(etaleTopology, AddCommGrpCat)} is

918	    \texttt{CategoryTheory.GrothendieckTopology.sheafify} (module

933	    \texttt{PicSharp.presheaf} and then forgetting from the sheaf

936	    AddCommGrpCat)}. The proof is structural: it identifies
937	    \texttt{etSheaf} (typed as a sheaf of sets via the concrete
938	    plus-construction
939	    \texttt{CategoryTheory.GrothendieckTopology.sheafify}) with the
940	    value of \texttt{presheafToSheaf etaleTopology AddCommGrpCat
941	    PicSharp.presheaf} under the forgetful functor, using
942	    \texttt{CategoryTheory.GrothendieckTopology.sheafification\_obj}
943	    (the equality \(\texttt{J.sheafification D}.\mathrm{obj}\, P =
944	    \texttt{J.sheafify}\, P\)) and the fact that the forgetful functor
945	    \(\texttt{AddCommGrpCat} \to \mathbf{Set}\) preserves the
946	    multiequalizer / filtered-colimit data used by the
947	    plus-construction (\texttt{plusPlusSheaf} commutes with

949	\end{itemize}
950	
951	The relationship with \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability} is captured by
952	the slogan: the FGA Picard scheme of A.2.c represents
953	\(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) (Kleiman \S 4, th:main: ``\dots represents
954	\(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\)''), \emph{not} \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) directly. The \'etale
955	sheafification step of this chapter is precisely what makes the
956	representability theorem in A.2.c well-posed.

958	\section{Out of scope}
959	\label{sec:relpic_out_of_scope}
960	
961	The following constructions are downstream of this chapter and live in
962	dedicated sibling chapters; this chapter does \emph{not} touch them:
963	\begin{itemize}
964	  \item \textbf{Representability of \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) by a scheme}
965	    (A.2.c, \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}) --- the
966	    Grothendieck--Mumford--Kleiman existence theorem is the next step but is
967	    not part of this chapter.

2	\chapter{Weil divisors on a smooth proper curve (RR.1)}
3	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}
4	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II \S6, pp.~130--137 +
5	% IV.1, pp.~294--296 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).
6	% Stacks pointers (tag-level, not verbatim-quoted): 02RW (divisors),
7	% 02ME (order at a point on a regular 1-dimensional scheme),
8	% 0BE0 (degree of a divisor on a curve), 0BE3 (principal divisors have
9	% degree zero on a complete nonsingular curve).
10	\section{Setup and motivation}
11	This chapter is the first of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
12	that close the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
13	\[
14	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
15	  \,\colon\,
16	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
17	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
18	\]
19	The bridge as proved in Hartshorne (Example~IV.1.3.5) chains together:
20	divisor of a closed point on a curve (\texttt{RR.1}, this chapter); the
21	Riemann--Roch dimension formula \(\ell(D) = \deg D + 1\) for \(\deg D \geq 0\) on
22	a genus-\(0\) curve (\texttt{RR.2}); the global-sections argument identifying
23	\(\dim_k H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) via \(1\) and a non-constant function
24	(\texttt{RR.3}); the ``rational curve \(\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1\)''
25	classification recovered as a degree-\(1\) morphism into \(\mathbb P^1\) via
26	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
27	Mathlib ships no \texttt{WeilDivisor} on a scheme. Adjacent pieces exist, but
28	none of them is the data structure we need: \texttt{MeromorphicOn.divisor}
29	(\texttt{Mathlib.Analysis.Meromorphic.Divisor}) is the order function of a
30	meromorphic function valued in a \emph{normed} field, not a formal sum of
31	codimension-\(1\) cycles on a scheme; \texttt{CommRing.Pic}
32	(\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) is the ring-level Picard group of
33	invertible modules under tensor product, with no scheme-level analogue;
34	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap}
35	(\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Birational.RationalMap}) packages rational
36	maps but not divisors. Consequently this chapter, and the three siblings, are
37	\emph{project-bespoke}: the only Mathlib tools they rely on are
38	discrete-valuation rings, the function field, and the ambient scheme API.
39	The audit grounding this scope decision is in
40	\texttt{analogies/rrbridge-survey.md}.
41	This chapter establishes the formal-sum data type
42	\(\mathrm{Div}(X) = \bigoplus_{\substack{Z \subset X \\ \mathrm{codim} 1}} \mathbb Z\) on a
43	Noetherian integral scheme \(X\) satisfying Hartshorne's condition \((*)\), the
44	principal-divisor homomorphism \(\mathrm{div} \colon K(X)^{\times} \to \mathrm{Div}(X)\) on a
45	curve, the degree map \(\deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C) \to \mathbb Z\) on a smooth proper
46	curve over an algebraically closed field, and the linear-equivalence
47	relation. The \emph{degree-zero of a principal divisor} statement
48	(\ref{thm:principal_deg_zero}) is included for completeness; its proof
49	decomposes into a separate sub-build inside \texttt{RR.1} (it uses the
50	auxiliary lemma that any non-constant rational function on \(C\) defines a
51	finite morphism \(C \to \mathbb P^1\)). The line-bundle construction $\mathcal
52	O_C(D)\( and the associated cohomology computation \)H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))$
53	live in the sibling chapter \texttt{RR.3}; the Riemann--Roch dimension
54	formula lives in \texttt{RR.2}; the genus-\(0\) classification proper lives in
55	\texttt{RR.4}.
56	Throughout this chapter, the standing convention is Hartshorne's condition
57	\((*)\):
58	% SOURCE QUOTE: "\((\ast)\) \(X\) is a noetherian integral separated scheme which
59	% is regular in codimension one."
60	\textit{Source: Hartshorne, II \S6, p.~130, the conditional \((*)\).}
61	\[
62	  (\ast)\qquad
63	  X \text{ is a noetherian integral separated scheme which is regular in
64	  codimension one.}
65	\]
66	For the degree map and for principal-divisor statements on a curve, we
67	additionally assume \(X\) is a complete (i.e.\ proper) nonsingular curve over
68	an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), where Hartshorne's condition \((*)\) is
69	automatic.
70	\paragraph{Standing hypothesis \((*)\) in the Lean encoding (iter-173 pin).}
71	Mathlib (as of \texttt{b80f227}) does not bundle Hartshorne's four-clause
72	\((\ast)\) as a single typeclass. The chapter pins \((*)\) to a \emph{layered}
73	typeclass set that the prover-agent threads through the Lean signatures in
74	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean}:
75	\begin{itemize}
76	  \item \textbf{Basic formal-sum layer} (carrier of \ref{def:prime_divisor} and
77	    \ref{def:codim1_cycles}, the additive group \(\mathrm{Div}(X)\), the degree
78	    \ref{def:divisor_degree}, the degree-homomorphism
79	    \ref{thm:divisor_degree_hom}, and \ref{def:divisor_closed_point}): the
80	    only Mathlib typeclass invoked is \texttt{[IsIntegral X]}
81	    (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme} via
82	    \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Properties}). Integrality of \(X\) already
83	    delivers \texttt{IrreducibleSpace X.carrier} (the carrier-level instance
84	    chain), so \(X\) has a unique generic point and the function field
85	    \texttt{X.functionField} (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.FunctionField})
86	    is a field; separatedness and noetherianness are \emph{not} required for
87	    the bare formal-sum data type.
88	  \item \textbf{Order/principal layer} (\ref{def:order_at_point},
89	    \ref{def:principal_divisor}, \ref{thm:principal_hom}): the Lean
90	    signature additionally threads
91	    \texttt{[IsLocallyNoetherian X]}
92	    (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Noetherian}) and Hartshorne's
93	    regular-in-codimension-one clause. The latter has \emph{no} Mathlib
94	    typeclass shipping it directly; the chapter's pin is the project-side
95	    predicate ``for every \(x \in X\) with \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{x\}} = 1\) the
96	    stalk \(\mathcal O_{X,x}\) is a discrete valuation ring'', encodable as
97	    the conjunction
98	    $\forall x,\; \texttt{Order.coheight}\,x = 1 \Rightarrow
99	    \texttt{IsDiscreteValuationRing}\,(\mathcal O_{X,x})$. Iter-173 introduced
100	    the \texttt{Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne} class

1	\chapter{Line-bundle pullback on a relative curve}
2	\label{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/LineBundlePullback.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-173). This chapter is the A.1.b sub-row of Route~A
6	% (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It packages the line-bundle
7	% pullback functor \(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) along the
8	% projection \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) of a relative curve, and the
9	% set-valued relative Picard presheaf
10	% \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) := \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\)
11	% as a functor on \((\Sch/k)^{op}\). This chapter is gated on
12	% \cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec} (iter-172) for the relative-spectrum machinery
13	% that any concrete Lean-side affine-cover argument will need, and it itself
14	% gates A.1.c (\'etale sheafification / \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) as a sheaf), A.2.*
15	% (Quot / Hilbert representability), A.3 (identity component), and A.4
16	% (universal property = Albanese / Jacobian).
17	
18	% NOTE (planner directive iter-173). Per the iter-173 blueprint-review
19	% recommendation (see .archon/task_results/blueprint-reviewer-route173.md,
20	% sub-phase choice), this chapter defines \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) as a
21	% \emph{set-valued} presheaf on \((\Sch/k)^{op}\). The abelian-group refinement
22	% (and the \'etale-sheafification step) belong to the follow-up chapter
23	% Picard_RelPicFunctor.tex (A.1.c). The set-valued formulation lets us discharge
24	% well-definedness of the quotient and functoriality of \(g^*\) as soon as a
25	% line-bundle pullback functor and its composition identity are in hand, with
26	% no reliance on Mathlib's \texttt{AddCommGrp} \'etale-sheafification API which
27	% does not yet exist.
28	
29	\section{Setup and motivation}
30	\label{sec:lbp_setup}
31	
32	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically
33	integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) (the curve carrying the Jacobian; the chapter's
34	constructions are functorial in \(C\) and do not formally use these hypotheses
35	until they meet the representability theorems in the downstream A.1.c / A.2.*
36	chapters). A test object is a \(k\)-scheme \(T\); the relative curve over \(T\) is
37	\(C \times_k T \xrightarrow{\pi_T} T\), with \(\pi_C : C \times_k T \to C\) the
38	companion projection. Both \(\pi_T\) and \(\pi_C\) are flat (because base change of
39	flat morphisms is flat and \(C/k\) is flat since \(k\) is a field).
40	
41	The relative Picard presheaf compares two natural line-bundle groups on the
42	product: the Picard group \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) of the total space, and the
43	Picard group \(\Pic(T)\) of the base. The pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\) sends a line
44	bundle on \(T\) to its base-change line bundle on \(C \times_k T\); the
45	\emph{relative Picard presheaf}
46	\[
47	  \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T)\, / \, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
48	\]
49	is the quotient set (and, with extra structure, the quotient abelian group). It
50	is the universal Set-valued functor on \((\Sch/k)^{op}\) that ``forgets line
51	bundles pulled back from the base'', and is the starting point of the
52	representability theorems of Grothendieck, Mumford, and Kleiman (see
53	\cref{chap:Jacobian} for the wider Route~A picture).
54	
55	\section{Line bundles on a relative curve}
56	\label{sec:lbp_line_bundle}

58	\begin{definition}\leanok

70	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,

72	  (cf.\ Stacks tag 01HK, Hartshorne II~§6).}
73	  Let \(k\) be a base field, \(C\) a \(k\)-scheme (in our application, a smooth proper
74	  geometrically integral curve), and \(T\) a \(k\)-scheme. A
75	  \emph{line bundle on the relative curve \(C \times_k T\)} is an invertible

79	  \(\mathcal{L}|_{U_i}\) is free of rank one. Two line bundles are isomorphic if
80	  they are isomorphic as \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)-modules. The set of

1	\chapter{Relative Spec}
2	\label{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-172). This chapter is the foundational A.1.a sub-row of
6	% Route A (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It packages the
7	% relative-spectrum functor \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) : \mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)^{op}
8	% \to \mathrm{Sch}/X used by the relative Picard functor on a product C \times_k T.
9	% Mathlib has the absolute Spec functor and affine gluing (Scheme.GlueData,
10	% AffineScheme.glueOpens) but no general RelativeSpec construction; this chapter is
11	% the prover-ready specification for the new file AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean
12	% which iter-173 (file-skeleton lane) will scaffold.
13	
14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	\label{sec:relspec_setup}
16	
17	The Route~A construction of \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety is
18	organised around the relative Picard functor
19	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\)
20	on the category of \(k\)-schemes. Representing \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) requires the line-bundle
21	pullback construction on a relative curve \(C \times_k T \to T\), which in turn rests on a
22	\emph{relative scheme} construction on a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras
23	\(\mathcal{A}\), the \emph{relative spectrum}
24	\(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\). This chapter packages the construction
25	of \(\underline{\Spec}_X(-)\) and its three core properties --- universal property, affine
26	base, base change --- as the foundational A.1.a building block. The relative Picard
27	functor itself (A.1.c), the line-bundle pullback functor on a product (A.1.b), and the
28	identity-component / dimension theory of \(J\) (A.3) live in separate sibling chapters
29	(\S~``Out of scope'' below).
30	
31	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section ``Relative spectrum via glueing'' and ``Relative
32	% spectrum as a functor'' (tags 01LL, 01LO, 01LQ, 01LR, 01LS, available locally at
33	% references/stacks-constructions.tex L309-L720). The Hartshorne reference is
34	% [Hartshorne], II Exercise 5.17 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).
35	
36	\section{Quasi-coherent sheaves of algebras}
37	\label{sec:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
38	
39	\begin{definition}\leanok
40	[Quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra]
41	  \label{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
42	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QcohAlgebra}
43	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section 01LL ``Relative spectrum via glueing'',
44	  % Situation ``situation-relative-spec''
45	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L312-L318).
46	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
47	  % "Here \(S\) is a scheme, and \(\mathcal{A}\) is a quasi-coherent
48	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebra. This means that \(\mathcal{A}\) is a
49	  %  sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebras which is quasi-coherent as an
50	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module."
51	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LL (situation-relative-spec).}
52	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. A \emph{quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras} is a
53	  sheaf \(\mathcal{A}\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras (i.e. an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
54	  equipped with \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear multiplication and unit morphisms satisfying
55	  associativity, commutativity, and the unit law as sheaf morphisms) whose underlying
56	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is quasi-coherent. We write \(\mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)\) for the
57	  category of quasi-coherent sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras and
58	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra morphisms.
59	\end{definition}

input.description: Check proof block of lem:degree_one_morphism_iso for uses{}

content:
% from `Module.finrank = 1`) closed axiom-clean inline iter-189 (~10 LOC via
% `Subalgebra.bot_eq_top_of_finrank_eq_one` + `Algebra.surjective_algebraMap_iff`
% + `RingHom.injective` + `RingEquiv.ofBijective`). Step 2 (scheme-level lift
% via `Scheme.Hom.toNormalization`) remains a typed sorry in the body, with 4
% sub-obligations (a)-(d) documented inline at the sorry. Iter-190+ closure
% budget ~80-150 LOC per `analogies/ratcurveiso-pin3.md`.
\begin{proof}



\leanok
  The function-field hypothesis \([K(C) : K(C')] = 1\) states that the
  canonical \(\varphi\)-induced map \(\varphi^\# \colon K(C') \to K(C)\) is a
  finite extension of degree one; since \(K(C')\) is a field and the
  extension has dimension one as a \(K(C')\)-vector space, \(\varphi^\#\) is
  an isomorphism of function fields \(K(C') \cong K(C)\). (Equivalently, by
  the IV.2-opening identification \(\deg(\varphi) = [K(C):K(C')]\), this is
  the statement \(\deg(\varphi) = 1\); the morphism \(\varphi\) is finite in
  this case by the standard argument that a non-constant morphism of
  smooth proper curves is finite, identical to the
  \texttt{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor} preamble.)
  By Hartshorne's Corollary~I.6.12, the assignment \(C \mapsto \bar k(C)\) is
  an equivalence of categories between smooth projective curves over $\bar
  k$ (with dominant morphisms) and function fields of transcendence degree
  one over \(\bar k\) (with \(\bar k\)-homomorphisms). Under this equivalence,
  the isomorphism \(\bar k(C') \cong \bar k(C)\) produced by the previous
  paragraph lifts to a unique isomorphism of smooth proper curves $C \to
  C'\(, and this isomorphism agrees with \)\varphi$ on the generic point (by
  construction: it is the inverse-image of \(\varphi^\#\) under the
  equivalence). The two morphisms \(C \to C'\) that agree on the generic
  point of the integral scheme \(C\) agree everywhere (a morphism out of an
  integral scheme to a separated scheme is determined by its restriction
  to the generic point, Hartshorne~II Ex.~4.2). Hence \(\varphi\) is itself
  the lift, and is therefore an isomorphism.
  \emph{Alternative direct argument.} Without invoking the
  equivalence-of-categories framing, the same conclusion is obtained
  scheme-theoretically: a finite degree-\(1\) morphism $\varphi \colon C \to
  C'$ between integral normal noetherian schemes is an isomorphism.
  Indeed, the direct image \(\varphi_* \mathcal O_C\) is a coherent
  \(\mathcal O_{C'}\)-module that is generically of rank $\deg(\varphi) =
  1\(, and an inclusion \)\mathcal O_{C'} \hookrightarrow \varphi_*
  \mathcal O_C\( of coherent rank-\)1$ sheaves whose generic ranks agree
  is an isomorphism over the locus where the quotient
  \(\varphi_* \mathcal O_C / \mathcal O_{C'}\) has trivial stalk. The
  quotient is a torsion coherent \(\mathcal O_{C'}\)-module of generic rank
  \(\deg(\varphi) - 1 = 0\), hence is itself torsion-free (a
  finitely-generated torsion module over a Dedekind domain of generic
  rank zero is zero), so vanishes. Therefore $\varphi_* \mathcal O_C =
  \mathcal O_{C'}\( and \)\varphi$ is the structure morphism of an
  isomorphism. Cf.\ Stacks tag~\textbf{0AVX} for the categorical version.
  \emph{Sub-build note.} The Lean signature
  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.iso\_of\_degree\_one} consumes a
  non-constant morphism \(\varphi \colon C \to C'\) together with a
  \texttt{[Algebra C'.functionField C.functionField]} instance argument
  (the canonical \(\varphi\)-induced function-field map) and the
  function-field-extension-degree-one hypothesis
  \texttt{Module.finrank C'.functionField C.functionField = 1}, and
  produces an isomorphism \(C \cong C'\) in the slice category
  \(\mathtt{Over (Spec (.of\ kbar))}\). The proof body threads the Mathlib
  API \texttt{IsIntegralHom.iso\_of\_isUnit} (or the equivalent
  \texttt{IsAffineHom + Finite + RankOne} chain) and, where needed, the
  project-bespoke ``finite-morphism-of-degree-one is an isomorphism''
  helper. The Hartshorne~I.6.12 equivalence-of-categories framing is the
  intended high-level proof structure; the scheme-theoretic argument
  above is the spelled-out form for the Lean body.
\end{proof}
\subsection{The normalization factorisation helpers (Lean substrate)}
% The five blocks of this subsection give 1-to-1 blueprint coverage for the
% Lean helper declarations into which the body of `lem:degree_one_morphism_iso`
% (`iso_of_degree_one`) decomposes: the bijectivity of the function-field
% algebra map under finrank one, the induced function-field iso, local

But as I noted above, they have out-edges themselves (each points to at least one other declaration), so they are not isolated.

Now, the question is: are `lem:phi_left_toNormalization_isIso_of_isIntegralHom` and `lem:phi_left_fromNormalization_isIso_of_smoothProper_finrank_one` purely leaf nodes (no in-edges from any statement-level `\uses{}`)? From my analysis:


- `lem:phi_left_toNormalization_isIso_of_isIntegralHom` depends on `lem:phi_left_locallyQuasiFinite_of_finrank_one`, and nothing in any chapter statement-uses this lemma.
- `lem:phi_left_fromNormalization_isIso_of_smoothProper_finrank_one` depends on `def:phi_left_functionField_algEquiv_of_finrank_one`, and nothing in any chapter statement-uses this definition either.

So both are leaf nodes in the reverse dependency direction, meaning the statement-level `\uses{}` in `lem:degree_one_morphism_iso` doesn't actually connect to them.

The proof of `lem:degree_one_morphism_iso` chains these lemmas together, but since leandag only builds edges from statement-level `\uses{}`, they're effectively disconnected from the consumed-by chain at the statement level.     status = 'UNMATCHED' if name in unmatched_set else 'OK'
    print(f'{status}: {name}')
"
